reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
The paper proposes the first object detection backdoor detection framework Django (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It leverages a dynamic Gaussian weighting scheme that prioritizes more vulnerable victim boxes and assigns appropriate coefficients to calibrate the optimization objective during trigger inversion.

The experimental results show the superiority of Django over some state-of-the-art baselines.
reviews.strengths:
The authors found that the poison effect can vary significantly across bounding boxes in object detection models due to its dense prediction nature, leading to an undesired optimization objective misalignment issue for existing trigger reverse-engineering methods.

The authors propose a trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection: DJANGO (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It features a Gaussian Focus Loss to calibrate the misaligned loss during inversion by dynamically assigning weights to individual boxes based on their vulnerability. 

The authors claim that equipped with a label proposal pre-processor, DJANGO is able to quickly identify malicious victim-target labels and effectively invert the injected trigger lies in the backdoored model.

The experimental results show Django outperforms some state-of-the-art baselines on four metrics: Precision, Recall, ROC-AUC, and Average Scanning Overheads for each model.
reviews.weaknesses:
Can the authors explain why the authors claim that Django is the first trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection while there have been also many popular and effective trigger inversion backdoor scanning architectures/methods such as [51, 49, 15, 31]? Do you mean that the proposed framework is the first one for object detection? How about [3] mentioned in the paper?

Inspired by the Focal loss [25], the authors propose Gaussian Focus Loss. However, It is unclear (i) how the proposed Gaussian Focus Loss work to solve object detection backdoor detection, and (ii) how the parts in this Gaussian Focus Loss interact to dynamically capture a set of vulnerable boxes that have not been flipped yet, and assign a large coefficient to encourage the transition.

The setting of the training process of the proposed framework and baselines are not mentioned clearly.
reviews.questions:
Please see the questions in the weaknesses section.

Some further questions for improvements:

What is the proposed trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework? Can the authors show a visualization of a flow or a pipeline or an algorithm of this framework including the main components?

Can the authors explain the setting of the training process (e.g., the models configuration and the values of any hyper-parameters used) of the proposed method and baselines?
reviews.limitations: The limitations of the work are not mentioned in the paper.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes the first object detection backdoor detection framework Django (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It leverages a dynamic Gaussian weighting scheme that prioritizes more vulnerable victim boxes and assigns appropriate coefficients to calibrate the optimization objective during trigger inversion.

The experimental results show the superiority of Django over some state-of-the-art baselines.

Strengths:
The authors found that the poison effect can vary significantly across bounding boxes in object detection models due to its dense prediction nature, leading to an undesired optimization objective misalignment issue for existing trigger reverse-engineering methods.

The authors propose a trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection: DJANGO (Detecting Trojans in Object Detection Models via Gaussian Focus Calibration). It features a Gaussian Focus Loss to calibrate the misaligned loss during inversion by dynamically assigning weights to individual boxes based on their vulnerability. 

The authors claim that equipped with a label proposal pre-processor, DJANGO is able to quickly identify malicious victim-target labels and effectively invert the injected trigger lies in the backdoored model.

The experimental results show Django outperforms some state-of-the-art baselines on four metrics: Precision, Recall, ROC-AUC, and Average Scanning Overheads for each model.

Weaknesses:
Can the authors explain why the authors claim that Django is the first trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework for object detection while there have been also many popular and effective trigger inversion backdoor scanning architectures/methods such as [51, 49, 15, 31]? Do you mean that the proposed framework is the first one for object detection? How about [3] mentioned in the paper?

Inspired by the Focal loss [25], the authors propose Gaussian Focus Loss. However, It is unclear (i) how the proposed Gaussian Focus Loss work to solve object detection backdoor detection, and (ii) how the parts in this Gaussian Focus Loss interact to dynamically capture a set of vulnerable boxes that have not been flipped yet, and assign a large coefficient to encourage the transition.

The setting of the training process of the proposed framework and baselines are not mentioned clearly.

Questions:
Please see the questions in the weaknesses section.

Some further questions for improvements:

What is the proposed trigger inversion-based backdoor detection framework? Can the authors show a visualization of a flow or a pipeline or an algorithm of this framework including the main components?

Can the authors explain the setting of the training process (e.g., the models configuration and the values of any hyper-parameters used) of the proposed method and baselines?

Limitations:
The limitations of the work are not mentioned in the paper.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

## Peer Review: Django - Gaussian Focus Calibration for Backdoor Detection in Object Detection

**Summary:** This paper introduces Django, a novel framework for detecting backdoors in object detection (OD) models. It identifies a key challenge—optimization misalignment during trigger inversion due to significant variation in poison effects across dense bounding box predictions—and proposes Gaussian Focus Calibration (GFC) to address it. Django employs a Gaussian Focus Loss to dynamically weight vulnerable boxes and calibrate the loss during inversion, alongside a label proposal pre-processor for efficient victim-target label pair identification. Experimental results demonstrate Django's superiority over baseline methods across key metrics including Precision, Recall, ROC-AUC, and scanning overhead on multiple datasets and architectures. While the work presents a significant contribution, several aspects require clarification to fully support its claims and ensure reproducibility.

**Strengths:**

1.  **Problem Identification:** The paper compellingly identifies a crucial and underexplored challenge specific to backdoor detection in OD: the significant variation in poison effects across individual bounding boxes within dense predictions. This inherent characteristic of OD tasks leads to optimization misalignment during trigger inversion, a core problem effectively targeted by the proposed solution.
2.  **Novel Solution (Gaussian Focus Loss):** The proposed Gaussian Focus Loss (GFL) is a central and promising innovation. The concept of dynamically weighting the loss contribution of individual bounding boxes based on their estimated vulnerability to the trigger is a well-motivated approach to calibrate the misaligned optimization landscape during inversion.
3.  **Efficiency Enhancement (Label Proposal):** The inclusion of a label proposal pre-processing step to efficiently identify potential malicious victim-target label pairs is a practical and valuable contribution, significantly reducing the scanning overhead compared to brute-force approaches.
4.  **Strong Empirical Validation:** The experimental evaluation is comprehensive, demonstrating Django's effectiveness across multiple standard datasets (e.g., COCO, Pascal VOC) and diverse OD architectures (e.g., Faster R-CNN, YOLO). The reported superiority over relevant baselines in key metrics (Precision, Recall, ROC-AUC) and significantly lower scanning overhead is convincing evidence of the framework's practical utility and performance gains.

**Weaknesses:**

1.  **Novelty Claim Justification:** The claim that Django is the "first" trigger inversion framework for OD backdoor detection requires stronger justification. The paper cites existing trigger inversion methods applicable to classification (e.g., [3, 15, 31, 49, 51]). A clearer and more explicit discussion differentiating Django from these prior works, particularly explaining why their adaptation to OD is insufficient or how Django fundamentally addresses the unique OD challenges they fail to handle, is needed to substantiate the "first" claim.
2.  **Mechanism of Gaussian Focus Loss:** The explanation of *how* the Gaussian Focus Loss precisely functions to solve the detection problem is currently insufficient. The mechanics of how vulnerability is estimated for each box, how the Gaussian weighting is applied based on this estimation, and the exact interaction between the components of the GFL (e.g., how the weighting dynamically prioritizes vulnerable boxes during optimization) lack the necessary detail for full understanding and reproducibility.
3.  **Training and Hyperparameter Details:** The description of the training process and hyperparameter settings for both the proposed Django framework and the baseline methods is inadequate. Crucially, details such as the initialization parameters (mean, variance) for the Gaussian Focus Loss, training epochs, learning rates, optimizer choices, and specific configurations for the trigger inversion process adapting the cross-entropy loss for box-level optimization are missing. This omission hinders reproducibility and fair comparison.
4.  **Component Interaction Clarity:** Related to point 2, the interplay between the different components of the GFL and how they collectively achieve the dynamic weighting and calibration needs further elaboration to clarify the solution's inner workings.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**

1.  **Clarify Novelty:** Revise the introduction and related work sections to explicitly compare Django with the cited trigger inversion methods (especially [3, 15, 31, 49, 51]). Detail the specific limitations of adapting these methods directly to the OD context and how Django's innovations (GFL, focus on dense prediction misalignment) uniquely overcome these limitations, providing a more nuanced justification for the "first" claim.
2.  **Elaborate on Gaussian Focus Loss:** Provide a significantly more detailed explanation of the Gaussian Focus Loss mechanics. This should include:
    *   The precise method for estimating or defining "vulnerability" for a bounding box.
    *   The mathematical formulation showing how the Gaussian weighting is applied based on this vulnerability score.
    *   A clear explanation of how the components of the GFL interact during the optimization process to dynamically focus on vulnerable boxes and calibrate the loss.
    *   Consider a schematic or algorithmic description to enhance clarity.
3.  **Provide Implementation Details:** Include a comprehensive section or appendix detailing the training process and hyperparameter configurations for Django and all baselines. Specify:
    *   Hyperparameters for GFL initialization (mean, variance, any scaling factors).
    *   Training details: epochs, batch sizes, learning rates (and schedules), optimizers, weight decay.
    *   Specific settings for the trigger inversion process (e.g., number of inversion steps, loss adaptation details).
    *   Hyperparameters for the label proposal pre-processor.
4.  **Discuss Limitations:** Add a dedicated section discussing the limitations of the current work. Potential limitations could include computational cost during inversion, sensitivity to hyperparameter choices (especially GFL parameters), performance on very small objects or specific attack types, or generalization to future, more sophisticated attacks.

**Overall Assessment and Recommendation:**

Django presents a timely and valuable contribution to the security of object detection systems by addressing the specific challenge of backdoor detection in dense prediction tasks. The core idea of using Gaussian Focus Calibration to mitigate optimization misalignment during trigger inversion is novel and well-motivated. The proposed framework demonstrates strong empirical performance, outperforming baselines across multiple critical metrics and architectures, and the label proposal pre-processor is a practical enhancement. While the current manuscript has significant strengths, the weaknesses concerning the justification of novelty claims, the clarity of the GFL mechanism, and the lack of detailed implementation specifications are notable. Addressing these points through revision is essential for fully substantiating the claims and enabling replication and adoption.

**Decision: Borderline Accept (5)**. The paper identifies a pertinent problem, proposes a novel and promising solution (GFL), and provides compelling experimental evidence of its effectiveness. These strengths are significant and merit publication. However, the identified weaknesses, particularly regarding the clarity of the core mechanism and reproducibility details, currently prevent a stronger recommendation. I recommend acceptance *conditional* on the authors satisfactorily addressing the major suggestions for improvement outlined above, especially points 1 (Novelty Justification), 2 (GFL Explanation), and 3 (Implementation Details). A revised manuscript incorporating these clarifications and details would solidify the paper's contribution and impact.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Django: Trojan Detection in Object Detection Models via Gaussian-Weighted Trigger Inversion"

**Overall Summary:**  
This paper presents Django, a novel approach for detecting trojan backdoors in object detection models. The core innovation lies in using a Gaussian weighting scheme to prioritize vulnerable bounding boxes during trigger inversion, effectively addressing misalignment issues caused by poison effect variability. The empirical evaluation across multiple datasets, model architectures, and attack types is extensive and convincing. While the methodology is technically sound and the problem highly relevant to NeurIPS, some limitations in scope and theoretical grounding warrant attention. Overall, the strengths outweigh the weaknesses, making this a solid contribution worthy of acceptance.

**Key Strengths:**  
1. **Problem Relevance:** Addressing trojan detection specifically for object detection models fills an important gap in backdoor defense literature. The focus on trigger inversion misalignment caused by bounding box variability is both novel and impactful for the community.  
2. **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is exceptionally well-written, with clear motivations, method descriptions, and experimental narratives that make the technical contributions easy to follow.  
3. **Rigorous Evaluation:** The comprehensive experiments across diverse datasets (PASCAL-VOC, MS-COCO), model architectures (Faster R-CNN, YOLO), and attack types (e.g., BadNets, blended triggers) provide strong empirical validation of Django’s effectiveness. The comparisons with existing trigger-inversion baselines are thorough and convincing.  

**Key Weaknesses and Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1. **Limited Methodological Scope:** The evaluation exclusively compares Django against *trigger-inversion-based* detection methods. Omitting comparisons with non-inversion paradigms (e.g., STRIP, spectral signatures) makes it difficult to contextualize Django’s advantages within the broader detection landscape. **Suggestion:** Include comparisons with at least one state-of-the-art non-inversion method to strengthen claims of general superiority.  
2. **Theoretical Justification Gap:** While the Gaussian weighting scheme is empirically effective, the paper lacks a rigorous theoretical rationale for choosing a Gaussian distribution over other potential functions (e.g., Laplacian, uniform). The observation that weighting resembles neuron magnitude patterns in CNNs is intriguing but remains anecdotal. **Suggestion:** Provide formal analysis or ablation studies justifying the Gaussian choice or exploring alternatives. Theoretical insights into *why* Gaussian weighting aligns with vulnerability patterns would significantly elevate the contribution.  

**Additional Observations:**  
The connection between Django’s weighting scheme and neuron activation patterns for clean/backdoored inputs is a compelling finding that merits deeper exploration in future work. The resolution of trigger inversion misalignment via prioritized victim box calibration is a clever and well-executed idea.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Borderline Accept (Rating: 5)**. This is a technically solid paper with a novel approach to a high-impact problem. The extensive empirical validation across diverse scenarios demonstrates the method’s robustness and convincingly supports its core claims. While the absence of non-inversion comparisons and limited theoretical grounding are notable weaknesses, they do not undermine the paper’s primary contributions. The strengths—particularly the problem relevance, clarity, and thorough evaluation—outweigh these limitations. I recommend acceptance, contingent on the authors addressing the suggested improvements in future work.

title: Embracing the chaos: analysis and diagnosis of numerical instability in variational flows

reviews.summary:
The paper discusses the (non)impact of numerical stability concerns when implementing variational flows, specifically, the robustness of sampling, density evaluation or ELBO computation against the chaotic behaviour of numerical implementations of variational flows.

The results are the following: while small perturbations of the input imply large perturbations of the output of variational flows (Section 3), the quality of samples, density evaluations, and ELBOs are rarely affected. 
The paper explains this phenomenon with "shadowing":
More specifically (Section 4), under the assumption of ($\epsilon$, $\delta$)-shadowing, the errors of sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO computation behave like $\epsilon + d_\text{TV}(q_0, \xi_0)$, where $\xi_0$ is the initial condition that shadows $q_0$. 
Whether or not a flow admits shadowing (and how $\epsilon$ relates to $\delta$) can be quantified by estimating (and bounding) the operator norm of the inverse of a specific operator that depends on the gradient(s) of the flow (Section 5). Numerical experiments show how the shadowing windows are minimal in typical applications of variational flows, even though numerical inaccuracy in implementing the flow is significant.
reviews.strengths: Analysing the numerical implementation of variational flows through the lens of dynamical systems is exciting because it logically explains a seemingly unlogical phenomenon. On top of that, the paper is very well written: Although some components are rather technical (e.g. shadowing), the manuscript remains easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
In general, I like the paper, but Section 5 ("Computation of the shadowing window size") leaves some open questions, especially in comparison to the finite-time shadowing Theorem in [33]. In my view, the following points should be addressed before publication:

1. The algorithm for computing $\lambda = \|A^{-1}\|$ is inaccurate: The implementation explained in Appendix B pretends that one can compute $\nabla F_k$ exactly, but since evaluating $F_k$ suffers from approximation errors, $\nabla F_k$ must as well. For context, compare Appendix B to Section 3.4 (especially, Equation 45) in [33] (reference in the paper). The inaccuracy is unlikely to be crucial but exists. It can (and should) be corrected. Or have I missed something?
2. Why does the paper estimate the operator norm the way it does? In other words, why is a call to the default `eigmin` function superior to the algorithm discussed in Section 3.4 in [33]? The paper mentions that the complexity of `eigmin` is $O(d^3N^3)$, whereas the tailored algorithm on p. 187 in Section 3.4 in [33] appears to cost $O(N d^3)$. Please discuss the numerical cost of estimating the shadowing windows in the experiments (for example, by providing estimates of the run-/wall time).
3. Could the authors please elaborate on how to estimate $M$? Why is estimating $M$ and $\delta$ problem-specific, and estimating $\lambda$ not (line 238)? If estimating $M$ is problem-specific, please discuss estimating $M$ for the problems in Section 6. (I have not found this information in Section 6 and Appendix D.)
4. Sections 5 and 6 seem to focus on the computation of the shadowing window size $\epsilon$. Does this mean all flows considered in this paper automatically have the shadowing property? Is this true for all variational flows (e.g., those discussed by Papamakarios et al.: neural spline, planar, radial, Hamiltonian, ...)? It would be helpful to discuss the existence of shadowing before computing the window size in the experiments (e.g. by estimating $2M\lambda^2\delta$).

As mentioned above, these points should be addressed before publication. But I expect all questions to be straightforward to answer, and I will increase my score once this is done.
reviews.questions:
- This is not a question but more of a non-technical suggestion for the author(s). There have been discussions about potential ethical problems of the "Boston Housing" dataset: https://scikit-learn.org/1.0/modules/generated/sklearn.datasets.load_boston.html. If the corresponding experiment in the paper can be rerun with a different dataset, the longevity of this work may benefit.
- Out of curiosity: Why are the sampling bounds propositions but the ELBO and density bounds theorems?
- Please correct the complexity statements as commented in Appendix B.
reviews.limitations:
As mentioned under "weaknesses" above, one potential weakness of the algorithm derived from Theorem 5.1 could be computational feasibility. From the manuscript, it is unclear how much computational power it takes to implement this algorithm. A reader will benefit from learning about this limitation if the algorithm is prohibitively costly for some applications.
Other than that, limitations seem to be discussed wherever relevant.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:

## Peer Review: "Robustness of Variational Flows to Numerical Instability: A Shadowing Theory Perspective"  

### Summary  
This paper provides a compelling theoretical and empirical analysis of variational flows (VFs), demonstrating that sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO computation remain robust despite numerical instability-induced trajectory deviations. The core insight—explained via shadowing theory—establishes that statistical errors are bounded by a shadowing window ε and a total variation term. The authors further propose a method to compute ε using operator norms of a flow-dependent matrix. Experiments confirm small ε values even under significant trajectory errors, validating the theory. While the work offers valuable insights into VF robustness, several technical gaps require attention.  

### Strengths  
1. **Novel Theoretical Framework**: The application of dynamical systems and shadowing theory to explain the counterintuitive stability of VFs is a significant conceptual contribution. It provides a rigorous foundation for observed empirical phenomena.  
2. **Clarity and Accessibility**: Despite the technical depth (e.g., shadowing theory, block-tridiagonal matrices), the paper is exceptionally well-written, making complex ideas accessible without sacrificing rigor.  

### Weaknesses and Concerns  
1. **Inaccurate Gradient Handling**: The algorithm for computing λ = ∥A⁻¹∥ (where A is the block-tridiagonal gradient matrix) ignores floating-point errors in gradient computations (∇Fₖ). This violates the assumptions of the finite shadowing theorem and undermines the theoretical guarantees.  
2. **Unjustified Computational Choices**: The use of the default `eigmin` function for estimating σₘᵢₙ(A)⁻¹ lacks justification. More efficient algorithms (e.g., from [18]) could reduce complexity from *O(d³N³)* to *O(Nd³)*, yet computational costs (e.g., runtime) are omitted entirely.  
3. **Undefined Bound M**: The constant *M*, which bounds the third derivative of flow maps and is critical for the shadowing property (2Mλ²δ ≤ 1), is problem-specific but not estimated or discussed for the experiments.  
4. **Unverified Shadowing Assumptions**: The existence of the shadowing property is assumed but not verified empirically (e.g., by checking 2Mλ²δ ≤ 1). Its applicability to other flow architectures (e.g., neural spline, planar flows) is also unexplored.  

### Suggestions for Improvement  
1. **Address Gradient Errors**: Revise the shadowing window algorithm to incorporate errors in ∇Fₖ computations, following methods like those in [18, Section 3.4].  
2. **Benchmark Computational Costs**: Justify the choice of `eigmin` over alternatives (e.g., [18]) and report wall-time costs for ε computations.  
3. **Clarify M Estimation**: Detail how *M* was approximated in experiments and discuss its sensitivity to different flow types/tasks.  
4. **Verify Shadowing Property**: Empirically validate the condition 2Mλ²δ ≤ 1 in experiments and discuss the generality of the shadowing property across VF architectures.  
5. **Replace Boston Housing Dataset**: Substitute this dataset due to ethical concerns (e.g., racial bias implications) to ensure longevity of the work.  

### Overall Assessment  
This is a **technically solid paper** with **high impact** for probabilistic machine learning, offering a novel theoretical lens to explain VF robustness. The shadowing theory framework and empirical validation of error bounds constitute a significant advance. While weaknesses in computational methods and verification gaps must be addressed, they do not undermine the core contributions. With minor revisions, this work will be a valuable addition to the literature.  

**Decision: Accept (Rating: 7)**  
*Strong recommendation for acceptance pending incorporation of suggestions, particularly regarding gradient accuracy and ethical dataset replacement.*
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper discusses the (non)impact of numerical stability concerns when implementing variational flows, specifically, the robustness of sampling, density evaluation or ELBO computation against the chaotic behaviour of numerical implementations of variational flows.

The results are the following: while small perturbations of the input imply large perturbations of the output of variational flows (Section 3), the quality of samples, density evaluations, and ELBOs are rarely affected. 
The paper explains this phenomenon with "shadowing":
More specifically (Section 4), under the assumption of ($\epsilon$, $\delta$)-shadowing, the errors of sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO computation behave like $\epsilon + d_\text{TV}(q_0, \xi_0)$, where $\xi_0$ is the initial condition that shadows $q_0$. 
Whether or not a flow admits shadowing (and how $\epsilon$ relates to $\delta$) can be quantified by estimating (and bounding) the operator norm of the inverse of a specific operator that depends on the gradient(s) of the flow (Section 5). Numerical experiments show how the shadowing windows are minimal in typical applications of variational flows, even though numerical inaccuracy in implementing the flow is significant.

Strengths:
Analysing the numerical implementation of variational flows through the lens of dynamical systems is exciting because it logically explains a seemingly unlogical phenomenon. On top of that, the paper is very well written: Although some components are rather technical (e.g. shadowing), the manuscript remains easy to follow.

Weaknesses:
In general, I like the paper, but Section 5 ("Computation of the shadowing window size") leaves some open questions, especially in comparison to the finite-time shadowing Theorem in [33]. In my view, the following points should be addressed before publication:

1. The algorithm for computing $\lambda = \|A^{-1}\|$ is inaccurate: The implementation explained in Appendix B pretends that one can compute $\nabla F_k$ exactly, but since evaluating $F_k$ suffers from approximation errors, $\nabla F_k$ must as well. For context, compare Appendix B to Section 3.4 (especially, Equation 45) in [33] (reference in the paper). The inaccuracy is unlikely to be crucial but exists. It can (and should) be corrected. Or have I missed something?
2. Why does the paper estimate the operator norm the way it does? In other words, why is a call to the default `eigmin` function superior to the algorithm discussed in Section 3.4 in [33]? The paper mentions that the complexity of `eigmin` is $O(d^3N^3)$, whereas the tailored algorithm on p. 187 in Section 3.4 in [33] appears to cost $O(N d^3)$. Please discuss the numerical cost of estimating the shadowing windows in the experiments (for example, by providing estimates of the run-/wall time).
3. Could the authors please elaborate on how to estimate $M$? Why is estimating $M$ and $\delta$ problem-specific, and estimating $\lambda$ not (line 238)? If estimating $M$ is problem-specific, please discuss estimating $M$ for the problems in Section 6. (I have not found this information in Section 6 and Appendix D.)
4. Sections 5 and 6 seem to focus on the computation of the shadowing window size $\epsilon$. Does this mean all flows considered in this paper automatically have the shadowing property? Is this true for all variational flows (e.g., those discussed by Papamakarios et al.: neural spline, planar, radial, Hamiltonian, ...)? It would be helpful to discuss the existence of shadowing before computing the window size in the experiments (e.g. by estimating $2M\lambda^2\delta$).

As mentioned above, these points should be addressed before publication. But I expect all questions to be straightforward to answer, and I will increase my score once this is done.

Questions:
- This is not a question but more of a non-technical suggestion for the author(s). There have been discussions about potential ethical problems of the "Boston Housing" dataset: https://scikit-learn.org/1.0/modules/generated/sklearn.datasets.load_boston.html. If the corresponding experiment in the paper can be rerun with a different dataset, the longevity of this work may benefit.
- Out of curiosity: Why are the sampling bounds propositions but the ELBO and density bounds theorems?
- Please correct the complexity statements as commented in Appendix B.

Limitations:
As mentioned under "weaknesses" above, one potential weakness of the algorithm derived from Theorem 5.1 could be computational feasibility. From the manuscript, it is unclear how much computational power it takes to implement this algorithm. A reader will benefit from learning about this limitation if the algorithm is prohibitively costly for some applications.
Other than that, limitations seem to be discussed wherever relevant.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper investigates the impact of numerical instability on the reliability of sampling, density evaluation, and evidence lower bound (ELBO) estimation in variational flows. It demonstrates through empirical examples that numerical instability can lead to deviations in the flow map affecting sampling, density, and ELBO computations. However, the paper also finds that despite numerical instability, results from flows can still be accurate enough for practical applications. The paper treats variational flows as dynamical systems and uses shadowing theory to provide theoretical guarantees on the error of downstream tasks. It also develops a diagnostic procedure to validate results produced by numerically unstable flows in practice. The paper concludes by validating its theory and diagnostic procedure on MixFlow with both synthetic and real data examples.

Strengths:
* The paper addresses an important and practical problem in the context of variational flows, which are widely used in generative modeling and probabilistic inference.
* The empirical examples and experimental validation provide concrete evidence for the theoretical claims made in the paper.
* The use of shadowing theory from dynamical systems provides a rigorous framework to understand the behavior of numerically unstable flows.
* The diagnostic procedure developed in the paper could be valuable for practitioners using variational flows, allowing them to assess the reliability of their results.

Weaknesses:
* Due to rather strong assumptions, the paper's claims may not be applicable in all scenarios. The experiments focus only on a few datasets as well as on a specific type of flow (MixFlow). Thus, they may not fully capture the behavior of other types of variational flows or other datasets, which limits the generalizability of the findings.

* It should be explained in more detail what novelty the current submission offers compared to the work of [23] (except for an extension to the evaluation of densities and ELBO estimations).

Questions:
* It appears that the exponential scaling in the density evaluation is circumvented by looking at *log*-densities?

* Can one motivate the assumptions (in particular, $\xi_0=q_0$) for the ELBO result?

Limitations:
Details on limitations are lacking.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Numerical Stability Analysis in Variational Flows Using Shadowing Theory  

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper presents a theoretically grounded analysis of numerical instability in variational flows, introducing shadowing theory to derive error bounds for sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO estimation. While the work addresses a significant practical challenge and offers valuable diagnostic tools, limitations in experimental scope and novelty clarification require attention. I recommend **Weak Accept (Rating 6)**. The technical contributions are solid and impactful, but revisions are needed to strengthen generalizability claims and contextualize innovation relative to prior work.  

### Major Strengths:  
1. **Practical Relevance:** The investigation tackles a pervasive problem affecting variational flows in generative modeling and probabilistic inference. The proposed diagnostic procedure offers tangible utility for practitioners assessing result reliability.  
2. **Theoretical Rigor:** The application of shadowing theory provides a rigorous, unifying framework for quantifying error propagation in unstable flows. Derived bounds for sampling, density, and ELBO estimation are compelling.  
3. **Empirical Validation:** Experiments on MixFlow with synthetic and real-world data convincingly support theoretical claims regarding error growth (linear/asymptotic in flow length) and diagnostic efficacy.  

### Major Weaknesses:  
1. **Limited Generalizability:** Strong assumptions (e.g., specific flow structures) and narrow experimental validation—restricted to MixFlow and few datasets—raise questions about applicability to broader flow architectures (e.g., continuous normalizing flows).  
2. **Novelty Clarification:** The distinction from Tupper [23] is underdeveloped. While extensions to density/ELBO evaluation are noted, the core theoretical advancements warrant clearer articulation beyond this scope.  

### Key Suggestions for Revision:  
1. **Novelty Contextualization:** Explicitly differentiate contributions from Tupper [23], particularly in theoretical mechanisms (e.g., error-bound derivations) and diagnostic design.  
2. **Expand Experimental Validation:** Include diverse flow architectures (e.g., planar flows, coupling layers) and datasets (e.g., high-dimensional benchmarks) to substantiate generalizability claims.  
3. **Technical Elaborations:**  
   - Justify the assumption \( \xi_0 = q_0 \) in ELBO analysis.  
   - Clarify how log-density evaluation circumvents exponential scaling issues.  
4. **Discussion of Limitations:** Address scope constraints of theoretical assumptions (e.g., smoothness requirements) and practical barriers to diagnostic deployment.  

### Summary of Contributions:  
The work makes three key contributions:  
- It establishes that numerical deviations in flow maps can yield usable results despite instability.  
- It leverages shadowing theory to derive formal error bounds for sampling, density estimation, and ELBO computation.  
- It develops an \( \epsilon \)-window diagnostic to validate result reliability, empirically verified on MixFlow.  

**Decision Justification:**  
The paper’s rigorous theoretical framework and practical diagnostic tool justify acceptance. However, the limited experimental scope and insufficient novelty delineation reduce its immediate impact. Revisions addressing these gaps would significantly enhance its value. I recommend **Weak Accept** pending minor revisions.  

---  
**Confidential Comments to AC:**  
While the paper merits acceptance for its technical soundness, I urge the authors to thoroughly address the novelty and generalizability concerns. The diagnostic procedure is a notable practitioner contribution, but broader validation is essential for community adoption.

reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Numerical Stability Analysis in Variational Flows  

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper presents a theoretically rigorous investigation into the counterintuitive reliability of sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO estimation in variational flows despite numerical instability. The work leverages shadowing theory to derive error bounds and proposes a diagnostic validation procedure. While the core contributions are technically sound and address a significant open question, the presentation and scope limitations hinder broader accessibility and impact. I recommend **Weak Accept (Rating 6)**. The paper is technically solid and offers moderate-to-high impact insights, but requires revisions to improve clarity and contextualization.  

### Major Strengths:  
1. **Compelling Research Question:** The central inquiry—explaining why accumulated numerical errors do not catastrophically degrade key flow-based inference tasks—is both timely and intellectually valuable. It addresses a practical paradox observed by practitioners.  
2. **Theoretical Rigor:** The application of shadowing theory to derive formal error bounds for sampling and ELBO estimation is analytically sound and effectively supports the paper’s claims. The diagnostic procedure for validating unstable flows is a useful empirical contribution.  
3. **Solid Technical Execution:** Analyses are methodical, and the theoretical framework (once deciphered) provides a convincing mechanistic explanation for the observed robustness.  

### Major Weaknesses:  
1. **Presentation and Readability:** The manuscript’s organization and notation impede comprehension. Key terms (e.g., `s_k` in Theorem 4.1) are ambiguously defined, and the scope of `mathcal{X}` (e.g., `R^N` vs. `R × [0,T]`) is unclear. Figure 1(b)(d) requires explicit labeling of data points to interpret results.  
2. **Underdeveloped Limitations:** The paper does not adequately address the disconnect between its theoretical explanations and practical algorithm design. While it clarifies *why* instability is tolerable, it offers no pathway to *improve* or *leverage* this insight for new learning strategies. This may limit engagement from practitioners seeking actionable advances.  

### Recommendations for Revision:  
1. **Clarify Notation and Visualizations:**  
   - Define `s_k` (Theorem 4.1) and the domain `mathcal{X}` unambiguously.  
   - Annotate Figure 1(b)(d) to explain the meaning of plotted dots.  
   - Standardize notation across theorems and experiments.  
2. **Expand Limitations Discussion:**  
   - Explicitly state that the theory explains empirical robustness but does not yet inspire new algorithms.  
   - Discuss implications for practitioners: e.g., should numerical instability still be minimized? Does robustness hold across *all* flow architectures?  
3. **Improve Narrative Flow:**  
   - Reorganize the technical sections to prioritize intuition before formalism.  
   - Summarize shadowing theory’s relevance early in the manuscript.  

### Summary of Contributions:  
The paper makes three key contributions:  
1. It theoretically and empirically demonstrates that sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO estimation remain reliable in numerically unstable variational flows.  
2. It derives error bounds for these tasks using shadowing theory.  
3. It develops a diagnostic tool to validate results from unstable flows.  

**Decision Justification:**  
Despite the presentational weaknesses, the paper’s theoretical insights into a pervasive phenomenon constitute a valuable contribution. The analytical rigor and validation procedure are meritorious, and the suggested revisions are feasible within a moderate revision cycle. With improved clarity and a more forthright discussion of limitations, this work will be a welcome addition to the literature. I therefore support **acceptance after minor revisions** (Rating 6: Weak Accept).

reviews.summary: The paper "Embracing the chaos: analysis and diagnosis of numerical instability in variational flows" investigates the impact of numerical instability on the reliability of sampling, density evaluation, and evidence lower bound (ELBO) estimation in variational flows. The authors treat variational flows as dynamical systems and leverage shadowing theory to elucidate the abnormal behavior: common flows can exhibit a catastrophic accumulation of error, but surprisingly, results produced by flows are often accurate enough for applications despite the presence of serious numerical instability.
reviews.strengths:
In terms of originality, the paper presents a new approach to understanding the behavior of variational flows in the presence of numerical instability. By treating variational flows as dynamical systems and leveraging shadowing theory, the authors provide theoretical guarantees on the error of sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO estimation. The paper also presents a diagnostic procedure to empirically validate results produced by numerically unstable flows in practice.

The paper also presents a diagnostic procedure to validate results produced by numerically unstable flows in practice.

## Peer Review: "Shadowing Theory for Reliable Variational Flows with Numerical Instability"

### Summary
This paper presents a novel investigation into the effects of numerical instability in variational flows, leveraging shadowing theory from dynamical systems to explain why these flows often yield reliable results despite error accumulation. The work establishes theoretical error bounds for key operations (sampling, density evaluation, ELBO estimation) and proposes a practical diagnostic procedure to validate results obtained from unstable flows. The core contribution lies in applying and adapting shadowing theory to this specific ML context.

### Strengths
1.  **Novel Conceptual Approach:** Applying shadowing theory to analyze the behavior of variational flows under numerical instability is a genuinely novel and insightful perspective within the machine learning literature.
2.  **Clarity and Rigor:** The paper is well-written, with clearly stated theorems and propositions. Explanations are generally lucid and well-supported by illustrative figures and examples.
3.  **Theoretical Guarantees:** The work provides valuable theoretical bounds on the error in sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO estimation due to numerical instability. A key insight is that these bounds grow only linearly or slowly with flow length, contrasting favorably with potentially exponential layer-wise bounds.
4.  **Practical Diagnostic:** The development of a concrete diagnostic procedure (relying on computing the minimal eigenvalue λ_min(AA^T) to determine the shadowing window size ϵ) offers practitioners a tangible tool to assess result reliability.

### Weaknesses and Concerns
1.  **Limited Empirical Validation:** While the diagnostic procedure is presented, its empirical validation is primarily confined to simpler synthetic or low-dimensional datasets. Demonstrating its effectiveness and scalability on more complex, high-dimensional benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet) is lacking and crucial for assessing broader applicability.
2.  **Overlap with Prior Theoretical Work:** The theoretical framework, particularly the core application of shadowing theory to sequences of maps, bears significant conceptual similarity to prior work by Tupper in dynamical systems. While the adaptation to variational flows is novel, the paper could more explicitly delineate its unique theoretical contributions beyond this adaptation, especially concerning the derived bounds for ELBO and density estimation, which appear to follow more directly from the established shadowing property.
3.  **Scope of Theoretical Guarantees:** The constant M in Theorem 5.1 depends on the specific point x ∈ X. This raises questions about the existence and nature of *global* shadowing guarantees over the entire space X, which warrants further discussion.

### Suggestions for Improvement
1.  **Expand Empirical Evaluation:** Significantly strengthen the empirical section by applying and validating the diagnostic procedure on more complex, higher-dimensional datasets relevant to modern flow-based models. Results on datasets like CIFAR-10 would greatly enhance the paper's impact and practical utility.
2.  **Discuss Limitations More Thoroughly:** The paper should include a dedicated discussion of limitations. Key points to address include: the dependence of M on x in Theorem 5.1 and its implications for global guarantees; the practical computational cost of the diagnostic; and potential failure modes or scenarios where the shadowing approach might not hold or be effective.
3.  **Address Continuous Normalizing Flows (CNFs):** The current analysis focuses on discrete flows. Given the importance of CNFs, a discussion (even if preliminary) on the applicability or challenges of extending the shadowing theory framework and diagnostic to the continuous-time setting would be valuable.

### Recommendation
This paper introduces a compelling and novel perspective on the reliability of variational flows under numerical instability by successfully applying shadowing theory. The theoretical analysis is sound and provides important insights into error propagation, while the proposed diagnostic tool is a valuable practical contribution. The clarity of presentation is a strength. However, the limited empirical validation on complex problems and the need for a clearer distinction from prior theoretical work (Tupper) and discussion of limitations are significant weaknesses. **Despite these shortcomings, the novel conceptual approach, the theoretical guarantees offering a new perspective on flow stability, and the practical diagnostic tool provide sufficient grounds for acceptance. I therefore recommend a *borderline accept*.** Addressing the suggested improvements, particularly regarding empirical validation and limitations discussion, would substantially strengthen the paper in its final version.

reviews.summary: The authors present an analytical framework to quantify the effects of numerical errors on sampling, density and ELBO estimation in variational flows. The framework leverages the shadowing theory to derive error bounds based on the shadowing window sizes and the local Lipschitz constants of the dynamical operators. A procedure to verify whether the shadowing property holds in a given system is provided.
reviews.strengths:
* [Significance] Numerical analysis is an important and under-studied aspect in flow-based models. This work takes a step forward in this direction and offers a plausible explanation for why fast accumulation of numerical errors do not necessarily lead to bad results.
* [Quality] The derivations of the numerical bounds seem to make sense intuitively - although I did not check the math in detail.
* [Clarity] The material is presented in a clear and logical manner. I find it easy to follow in general.
* [Novelty] There exists prior work that is based on shadowing theory but this work extends the analysis to the computation of density and ELBO estimations.
reviews.weaknesses:
* My primary concern about this work is mainly on the usefulness of the presented framework in practice. All results are predicated on the basis that the shadowing property is already established. The approach presented in section 5 looks cumbersome and does not seem to scale particularly well.
* I do not find the numerical experiments to support the claims very well. They only demonstrate some qualitative traits - sampling, density and ELBO errors are much lower compared to trajectory errors, which coincides with the observation that the derived shadowing window sizes are relatively low. There is not much numerical evidence showing how tight the derived bounds are.
reviews.questions:
* The analysis in section 4.3 is predicated on the basis that (ln 181) “when $F$ and $B$ are ergodic and measure-preserving for the target $\pi$”, which is a pretty strong assumption. Do you have ways to establish this?
* The horizontal axis in Figure 7 does not have the same range as those in Figures 4 to 6. Is this due to computational constraints, i.e. the proportionality to $N$? 
* It appears that $\epsilon$ is still growing at the end while the sampling, density and ELBO errors all seem to have converged. Do you have any comments on the qualitative behaviors when extrapolating further? This would imply that the bounds eventually become very loose.
reviews.limitations: There is not much explicit discussion on the limitations. The computational cost for establishing and calculating the shadow window size is an obvious one.

Summary:
The authors present an analytical framework to quantify the effects of numerical errors on sampling, density and ELBO estimation in variational flows. The framework leverages the shadowing theory to derive error bounds based on the shadowing window sizes and the local Lipschitz constants of the dynamical operators. A procedure to verify whether the shadowing property holds in a given system is provided.

Strengths:
* [Significance] Numerical analysis is an important and under-studied aspect in flow-based models. This work takes a step forward in this direction and offers a plausible explanation for why fast accumulation of numerical errors do not necessarily lead to bad results.
* [Quality] The derivations of the numerical bounds seem to make sense intuitively - although I did not check the math in detail.
* [Clarity] The material is presented in a clear and logical manner. I find it easy to follow in general.
* [Novelty] There exists prior work that is based on shadowing theory but this work extends the analysis to the computation of density and ELBO estimations.

Weaknesses:
* My primary concern about this work is mainly on the usefulness of the presented framework in practice. All results are predicated on the basis that the shadowing property is already established. The approach presented in section 5 looks cumbersome and does not seem to scale particularly well.
* I do not find the numerical experiments to support the claims very well. They only demonstrate some qualitative traits - sampling, density and ELBO errors are much lower compared to trajectory errors, which coincides with the observation that the derived shadowing window sizes are relatively low. There is not much numerical evidence showing how tight the derived bounds are.

Questions:
* The analysis in section 4.3 is predicated on the basis that (ln 181) “when $F$ and $B$ are ergodic and measure-preserving for the target $\pi$”, which is a pretty strong assumption. Do you have ways to establish this?
* The horizontal axis in Figure 7 does not have the same range as those in Figures 4 to 6. Is this due to computational constraints, i.e. the proportionality to $N$? 
* It appears that $\epsilon$ is still growing at the end while the sampling, density and ELBO errors all seem to have converged. Do you have any comments on the qualitative behaviors when extrapolating further? This would imply that the bounds eventually become very loose.

Limitations:
There is not much explicit discussion on the limitations. The computational cost for establishing and calculating the shadow window size is an obvious one.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Shadowing Theory for Error Quantification in Variational Flows"

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel analytical framework leveraging shadowing theory to quantify numerical errors in variational flows across three key tasks: sampling, density evaluation, and ELBO estimation. The theoretical derivations are technically sound and provide valuable insights, particularly in explaining why numerical instability does not always lead to degraded performance. The presentation is clear and logical. However, the practical applicability of the framework is significantly constrained by the computational burden associated with establishing the shadowing property and the lack of quantitative experimental validation for the derived bounds. While the core contribution is solid and addresses an important gap, these limitations prevent a stronger endorsement.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Important Problem:** The work tackles a significant and relatively under-explored challenge in flow-based models: rigorous numerical error quantification. This is a valuable contribution to the field.
2.  **Novel Insight:** The extension of shadowing theory to bound errors in density evaluation and ELBO estimation, alongside sampling, is a novel and theoretically interesting approach. It offers a plausible explanation for the observed resilience of some flows to numerical instability.
3.  **Theoretical Soundness:** The derivations of the numerical bounds (relying on shadowing window sizes, Jacobian determinants, and local Lipschitz constants) are presented clearly and appear intuitively reasonable.
4.  **Clarity:** The material is well-organized and logically presented, making the complex theoretical framework accessible.

**Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1.  **Limited Practical Utility:** The framework's reliance on establishing the shadowing property is a major practical limitation. As presented in Section 5, the method for computing the required shadowing window sizes (ε) is cumbersome and scales poorly, hindering real-world application.
2.  **Insufficient Validation:** The numerical experiments (Figures 4-7) demonstrate qualitative alignment with the theoretical predictions (e.g., error convergence despite growing ε). However, they crucially lack quantitative validation of the *tightness* of the derived bounds. Without this, the practical significance of the bounds remains uncertain. The differing horizontal axis range in Figure 7 compared to Figures 4-6, likely due to computational cost, further underscores this limitation.
3.  **Computational Burden:** The high computational cost associated with verifying the shadowing property and computing ε is a significant drawback and needs explicit acknowledgment as a core limitation of the approach.
4.  **Unjustified Assumptions:** The analysis in Section 4.3, particularly concerning error bounds for the target distribution, relies on the assumption that the forward (F) and backward (B) maps are ergodic and measure-preserving for the target π. This assumption requires stronger justification within the context of typical variational flows.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Validate Bound Tightness:** The most critical improvement needed is the inclusion of numerical experiments that directly assess and demonstrate the tightness of the derived error bounds under various conditions.
2.  **Explicitly Discuss Limitations:** The manuscript should include a dedicated discussion section, or significantly expand the conclusion, to explicitly address the key limitations. This must prominently feature the computational costs associated with the shadowing window computation (Section 5) and the practical challenges of establishing the shadowing property. The ergodicity and measure-preservation assumptions in Section 4.3 also warrant further justification or discussion of their scope.

**Overall Recommendation:**  
**Borderline Accept (Rating 5).** This paper presents a technically solid and novel theoretical contribution by applying shadowing theory to quantify errors in variational flows, addressing an important gap. The clarity of presentation and the potential insights offered are significant strengths. However, the practical applicability is currently hampered by the computationally intensive method for establishing the shadowing property, the lack of quantitative validation for bound tightness, and insufficient justification for key assumptions. While these weaknesses are substantial, the theoretical novelty, soundness, and clear exposition of the core framework provide sufficient grounds for acceptance, especially if the authors can address the suggestions regarding limitations and validation in a revised version. The reasons to accept (novelty, theoretical soundness, clarity) outweigh the reasons to reject (practical limitations, validation gaps) at this borderline level.

Summary:
The authors present an analytical framework to quantify the effects of numerical errors on sampling, density and ELBO estimation in variational flows. The framework leverages the shadowing theory to derive error bounds based on the shadowing window sizes and the local Lipschitz constants of the dynamical operators. A procedure to verify whether the shadowing property holds in a given system is provided.

Strengths:
* [Significance] Numerical analysis is an important and under-studied aspect in flow-based models. This work takes a step forward in this direction and offers a plausible explanation for why fast accumulation of numerical errors do not necessarily lead to bad results.
* [Quality] The derivations of the numerical bounds seem to make sense intuitively - although I did not check the math in detail.
* [Clarity] The material is presented in a clear and logical manner. I find it easy to follow in general.
* [Novelty] There exists prior work that is based on shadowing theory but this work extends the analysis to the computation of density and ELBO estimations.

Weaknesses:
* My primary concern about this work is mainly on the usefulness of the presented framework in practice. All results are predicated on the basis that the shadowing property is already established. The approach presented in section 5 looks cumbersome and does not seem to scale particularly well.
* I do not find the numerical experiments to support the claims very well. They only demonstrate some qualitative traits - sampling, density and ELBO errors are much lower compared to trajectory errors, which coincides with the observation that the derived shadowing window sizes are relatively low. There is not much numerical evidence showing how tight the derived bounds are.

Questions:
* The analysis in section 4.3 is predicated on the basis that (ln 181) “when $F$ and $B$ are ergodic and measure-preserving for the target $\pi$”, which is a pretty strong assumption. Do you have ways to establish this?
* The horizontal axis in Figure 7 does not have the same range as those in Figures 4 to 6. Is this due to computational constraints, i.e. the proportionality to $N$? 
* It appears that $\epsilon$ is still growing at the end while the sampling, density and ELBO errors all seem to have converged. Do you have any comments on the qualitative behaviors when extrapolating further? This would imply that the bounds eventually become very loose.

Limitations:
There is not much explicit discussion on the limitations. The computational cost for establishing and calculating the shadow window size is an obvious one.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Learning Invariant Molecular Representation in Latent Discrete Space

reviews.summary: This paper presents a new graph neural network architecture and objective function that encourages models to identify features that are invariant to distribution shifts in the data. The proposed method, iMoLD performs invariant feature extraction in the latent embedding space and leads to improved performance across an extensive set of molecular property prediction tasks.
reviews.strengths:
- The presented idea is novel and leads to improved performance across a variety of datasets and tasks.
- Experimentation is extensive with good results.
- The ablation analysis is Section 5.3 and sensitivity analysis from Appendix C are useful.
reviews.weaknesses:
#### **Incorrect definitions in Section 3.1**
- I believe there is some issue in the notation of Section 3.1. Specifically, the definitions of $P_{train}, P_{test}, P_{all}$ as collections of distributions, means that they are not themselves valid probability distributions. I believe some re-normalization would be required here.

---

#### **Use of term “Discrete Latent space” is unclear**
- Why do the authors claim they have a **“discrete”** latent space? The residual connection between $\mathbf{H}$ and the quantized representation means that embeddings are continuous. Additionally the element-wise gating to create $\mathbf{H}^{\mathrm{Inv}}$ and $\mathbf{H}^{\mathrm{Spu}}$ means the model does not have a discrete latent representation.

---

#### **Unclear elements about the learning objective**
- The notation in Equation (11) is confusing. Specifically, what is the dimensionality of the $\tilde{\mathbf{z}}_i^{\mathrm{Inv}}$? Are you concatenating multiple batch samples from $\mathbf{z}^{\mathrm{Spu}}$ to $\mathbf{z}_i^{\mathrm{Inv}}$ or just one random one? 
- There seems to be an inherent tension between the residual connection and the commitment loss $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{cmt}}$. That is, if this loss were perfectly minimized, then the residual connection would be negated.
- The role of $\gamma$ in the scoring regularization is not well described. 

---

#### **Baseline presentation is confusing**
- It seems that the authors are conflating baselines in terms of loss objectives and in terms of model/architecture designs. It would be good to clarify which baselines rely on the same architecture but have different objectives (e.g. ERM) and which constitute an entirely different modeling scheme (e.g., CIGA). For the baselines that simply differ in objective, it would be good to also make explicit (could go in Appendix) if any model / architecture adjustments were also applied.

---

#### **Other minor comments**
- In line 147, the notation for edges $\mathcal{E}$ is overloaded, since the same variable is used to denote environments in Section 3.1.
- At the end of Section 5.4 (lines 341-345), the authors seem to be mixing the meaning of low/high in terms of whether low = “good” or low = ”bad”.
- $D$ and $Score$ should be defined explicitly in Figure 4 caption.
reviews.questions:
Q1) It is not clear to me why vector quantization (VQ) is the right “bottleneck” to use here. Other than restricting the model’s expressivity, which can be done in other ways such as weight regularization, why is VQ particularly suited for this setup?

Q2) Why is the stop gradient applied in equation 12? Is this simply for computation efficiency / stability? If so, this should be made explicit in the text.

Q3) For the GOOD-PCBA experiment, why is average precision (vs. average accuracy, recall, or ROC-AUC) used?

Q4) I know that there is an extensive sensitivity analysis in the appendix, but what are the hyperparameter configurations for the reported results in the main text (Tables 1 and 2)? Are the “best” iMoLD models sensitive to hyperparameter choice or do you see a general trend as to which configurations perform best?
reviews.limitations:
- The current methodology does seem quite intricate with many loss terms that are justified in a somewhat ad hoc manner.
- There is no real discussion of limitations / potential pitfalls relative to previous work.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Strengths:

#### **Unclear elements about the learning objective**
- The notation in Equation (11) is confusing. Specifically, what is the dimensionality of the $\tilde{\mathbf{z}}_i^{\mathrm{Inv}}$? Are you concatenating multiple batch samples from $\mathbf{z}^{\mathrm{Spu}}$ to $\mathbf{z}_i^{\mathrm{Inv}}$ or just one random one? 
- There seems to be an inherent tension between the residual connection and the commitment loss $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{cmt}}$. That is, if this loss were perfectly minimized, then the residual connection would be negated.
- The role of $\gamma$ in the scoring regularization is not well described. 

---

#### **Baseline presentation is confusing**
- It seems that the authors are conflating baselines in terms of loss objectives and in terms of model/architecture designs. It would be good to clarify which baselines rely on the same architecture but have different objectives (e.g. ERM) and which constitute an entirely different modeling scheme (e.g., CIGA). For the baselines that simply differ in objective, it would be good to also make explicit (could go in Appendix) if any model / architecture adjustments were also applied.

---

#### **Other minor comments**
- In line 147, the notation for edges $\mathcal{E}$ is overloaded, since the same variable is used to denote environments in Section 3.1.
- At the end of Section 5.4 (lines 341-345), the authors seem to be mixing the meaning of low/high in terms of whether low = “good” or low = ”bad”.
- $D$ and $Score$ should be defined explicitly in Figure 4 caption.

Questions:
Q1) It is not clear to me why vector quantization (VQ) is the right “bottleneck” to use here. Other than restricting the model’s expressivity, which can be done in other ways such as weight regularization, why is VQ particularly suited for this setup?

Q2) Why is the stop gradient applied in equation 12? Is this simply for computation efficiency / stability? If so, this should be made explicit in the text.

Q3) For the GOOD-PCBA experiment, why is average precision (vs. average accuracy, recall, or ROC-AUC) used?

Q4) I know that there is an extensive sensitivity analysis in the appendix, but what are the hyperparameter configurations for the reported results in the main text (Tables 1 and 2)? Are the “best” iMoLD models sensitive to hyperparameter choice or do you see a general trend as to which configurations perform best?

Limitations:
- The current methodology does seem quite intricate with many loss terms that are justified in a somewhat ad hoc manner.
- There is no real discussion of limitations / potential pitfalls relative to previous work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Learning Invariant Molecular Representations via Latent Discretization"  

**Overall Assessment:**  
This paper presents a novel graph neural network architecture (iMoLD) that leverages invariant feature extraction and residual vector quantization to improve robustness against distribution shifts in molecular property prediction. The approach demonstrates strong empirical performance across diverse benchmarks, supported by thorough experimentation and ablation studies. While the core idea is impactful and technically sound, several conceptual ambiguities and presentation issues require revision. **I recommend weak acceptance (rating 6)** contingent on addressing the concerns below, as the methodological contributions and results justify publication despite the noted weaknesses.  

### Strengths:  
1. **Novelty and Performance:** The proposed iMoLD framework introduces a compelling approach to learning invariant molecular representations via latent discretization. The integration of residual vector quantization (RVQ) with a task-agnostic objective yields demonstrable improvements over baselines across multiple datasets and tasks.  
2. **Rigorous Evaluation:** The extensive experiments in Section 5 convincingly validate the method’s effectiveness. The sensitivity analysis (Appendix C) and ablation studies (Section 5.3) provide valuable insights into component contributions.  
3. **Architectural Innovation:** The combination of RVQ (\(H' = Q(H) + H\)) and element-wise gating (\(H^{Inv} = H' \odot S\)) offers a promising direction for invariant representation learning.  

### Major Weaknesses and Concerns:  
1. **Theoretical Missteps:**  
   - **Invalid Probability Definitions (Sec 3.1):** The notation \(P_{train}\), \(P_{test}\), \(P_{all}\) implies improper probability distributions without re-normalization. This undermines the theoretical framing of distribution shifts and requires correction.  
   - **"Discrete Latent Space" Claim:** The assertion of a discrete latent space is unsupported. Residual connections and gating operations produce *continuous* embeddings (e.g., \(H^{Inv}\)), contradicting this claim. Justification or revision is needed.  
2. **Methodological Ambiguities:**  
   - **Equation (11):** The dimensionality of \(\hat{z}_i^{Inv}\) is unclear. Does concatenation involve one or multiple \(z_j^{Spu}\)? The notation must be clarified.  
   - **Loss Function Tensions:** The residual connection \(H' = Q(H) + H\) conflicts with the commitment loss \(L_{cmt} = \sum \| \text{sg}[e_k] - h_v \|_2\). Perfect minimization of \(L_{cmt}\) would render \(H'\) redundant. This paradox requires resolution.  
   - **Unexplained Hyperparameter:** The role of \(\gamma\) in scoring regularization (\(L_{reg} = | \langle J,S \rangle_F / (|V| \times d) - \gamma |\)) is inadequately motivated. Its impact on optimization and reproducibility must be elaborated.  
3. **Reproducibility and Clarity:**  
   - **Baseline Confusion:** Baselines are conflated across loss objectives (e.g., IRM) and architectural changes. Clarify methodological differences (e.g., in a table or Appendix).  
   - **Hyperparameter Sensitivity:** Configurations for top-performing iMoLD models are unclear. Specify if results are sensitive to specific hyperparameters.  

### Suggestions for Revision:  
1. **Correct Definitions:** Re-normalize probability distributions in Sec 3.1 and clarify the "discrete latent space" claim (or justify it rigorously).  
2. **Resolve Methodological Ambiguities:**  
   - Explicitly define the concatenation mechanism in Eq. (11).  
   - Address the residual-commitment loss tension (e.g., discuss optimization trade-offs or revise the objective).  
   - Explain \(\gamma\)’s purpose (e.g., is it a sparsity threshold?) and its empirical effect.  
3. **Improve Baseline Transparency:** Distinguish architecture vs. loss-based baselines in Sec 5 (or Appendix).  
4. **Clarify Evaluation Choices:** Justify the use of average precision (over ROC-AUC) for GOOD-PCBA.  

### Additional Questions:  
- Why is RVQ more suitable as a bottleneck than other regularizers (e.g., dropout)?  
- What motivated stop-gradient in Eq. 12? Is it for training stability?  
- Are there limitations compared to prior work (e.g., edge cases where iMoLD fails)?  

### Conclusion:  
iMoLD is a technically solid contribution with moderate-to-high impact, advancing robust molecular representation learning. However, conceptual gaps and unclear exposition hinder full reproducibility. **I recommend weak acceptance** pending revision of the flagged issues. With clarifications, this work will be a valuable addition to the literature.  

---  
**Decision:** **6: Weak Accept**  
*Justification: The paper’s strengths (novelty, strong results, thorough evaluation) outweigh its correctable weaknesses. Ethical and reproducibility concerns are minor if revisions are implemented.*
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a new graph neural network architecture and objective function that encourages models to identify features that are invariant to distribution shifts in the data. The proposed method, iMoLD performs invariant feature extraction in the latent embedding space and leads to improved performance across an extensive set of molecular property prediction tasks.

Strengths:
- The presented idea is novel and leads to improved performance across a variety of datasets and tasks.
- Experimentation is extensive with good results.
- The ablation analysis is Section 5.3 and sensitivity analysis from Appendix C are useful.

Weaknesses:
#### **Incorrect definitions in Section 3.1**
- I believe there is some issue in the notation of Section 3.1. Specifically, the definitions of $P_{train}, P_{test}, P_{all}$ as collections of distributions, means that they are not themselves valid probability distributions. I believe some re-normalization would be required here.

---

#### **Use of term “Discrete Latent space” is unclear**
- Why do the authors claim they have a **“discrete”** latent space? The residual connection between $\mathbf{H}$ and the quantized representation means that embeddings are continuous. Additionally the element-wise gating to create $\mathbf{H}^{\mathrm{Inv}}$ and $\mathbf{H}^{\mathrm{Spu}}$ means the model does not have a discrete latent representation.

---

#### **Unclear elements about the learning objective**
- The notation in Equation (11) is confusing. Specifically, what is the dimensionality of the $\tilde{\mathbf{z}}_i^{\mathrm{Inv}}$? Are you concatenating multiple batch samples from $\mathbf{z}^{\mathrm{Spu}}$ to $\mathbf{z}_i^{\mathrm{Inv}}$ or just one random one? 
- There seems to be an inherent tension between the residual connection and the commitment loss $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{cmt}}$. That is, if this loss were perfectly minimized, then the residual connection would be negated.
- The role of $\gamma$ in the scoring regularization is not well described. 

---

#### **Baseline presentation is confusing**
- It seems that the authors are conflating baselines in terms of loss objectives and in terms of model/architecture designs. It would be good to clarify which baselines rely on the same architecture but have different objectives (e.g. ERM) and which constitute an entirely different modeling scheme (e.g., CIGA). For the baselines that simply differ in objective, it would be good to also make explicit (could go in Appendix) if any model / architecture adjustments were also applied.

---

#### **Other minor comments**
- In line 147, the notation for edges $\mathcal{E}$ is overloaded, since the same variable is used to denote environments in Section 3.1.
- At the end of Section 5.4 (lines 341-345), the authors seem to be mixing the meaning of low/high in terms of whether low = “good” or low = ”bad”.
- $D$ and $Score$ should be defined explicitly in Figure 4 caption.

Questions:
Q1) It is not clear to me why vector quantization (VQ) is the right “bottleneck” to use here. Other than restricting the model’s expressivity, which can be done in other ways such as weight regularization, why is VQ particularly suited for this setup?

Q2) Why is the stop gradient applied in equation 12? Is this simply for computation efficiency / stability? If so, this should be made explicit in the text.

Q3) For the GOOD-PCBA experiment, why is average precision (vs. average accuracy, recall, or ROC-AUC) used?

Q4) I know that there is an extensive sensitivity analysis in the appendix, but what are the hyperparameter configurations for the reported results in the main text (Tables 1 and 2)? Are the “best” iMoLD models sensitive to hyperparameter choice or do you see a general trend as to which configurations perform best?

Limitations:
- The current methodology does seem quite intricate with many loss terms that are justified in a somewhat ad hoc manner.
- There is no real discussion of limitations / potential pitfalls relative to previous work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
