reviews.original_rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary:
This manuscript proves that an embedding with polynomial width in the set size and feature dimension is sufficient to precisely reconstruct a set function, under some constraints on the embedding layer architecture. The main contribution is on the upper bound, which removes assumptions of previous studies, and reduces the gap exponentially. The scope of this study covers permutation-invariant and permutation-equivariant set functions in high-dim scenarios. 

The key idea of the proof is to construct the embedding vector of the claimed length, and show the injectivity of any input set elements with the proposed embedding layers. The weights are used to (1) save the original features; (2) form an 'anchor' of the set element, which 'shares' the same permutation with the set element features; (3) store the coefficients that mix the original feature and the anchor.
reviews.strengths:
- The manuscript is clearly written. The problem is sound and well-motivated.
- The results are significant, extending previous study to high-dimensions and exact representation of sets, in addition to tightening the upper bound from exponential to polynomial.
reviews.weaknesses:
The major weakness of this study has been summarized in the limitation section already: (1) There are two important parameters in DeepSet, however only one is considered. (2) The lower bounds are trivial. (3) The upper bound depends on the specific neural network architecture.

Another issue is the proof is an existential argument. That is if we find those weights, the network can precisely represent the set function. However it is not clear if the weights belong to some space as a convergence by training the network. If the authors could provide intuition on this it would be good.

It took me longer than expected to understand the functionality of the three segments of the weights, especially the third one. I would suggest one more paragraph describing the mentality of the construction, together with the Eq 5,6,7.

From the technique side, there is no novel technique involved, and this together with the major weakness would be my personal reasonable potential to reject this paper. But fairly speaking I think the depth is OK, and the results are sufficiently impressive. Thus I recommend accept.
reviews.questions:
- Line 223 and 340 indicate the lower bound of $L\geq ND$ for the LLE architecture, it may be better to add to line 63.
- In DeepSets, the $\rho$ and $\phi$ function can be implemented by several neural network layers of choice. Is function $\phi$ narrowed down to either 2 layers (LP) or 3 layers (LLE)? Or I believe at least the "linear" layer can be extended to multiple fully-connected layers. More generally, what is the flexibility in the network design during the implementation?
- This is out of curiosity only, I think it would be more intrigued to study the unconditional lower bound of this problem. Theorem 2.4 seems to address this when $D=1$. What is the barrier of obtaining an $\Omega(f(N,D))$ for general $D$?
reviews.limitations: Mentioned above.
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

Summary:
This manuscript proves that an embedding with polynomial width in the set size and feature dimension is sufficient to precisely reconstruct a set function, under some constraints on the embedding layer architecture. The main contribution is on the upper bound, which removes assumptions of previous studies, and reduces the gap exponentially. The scope of this study covers permutation-invariant and permutation-equivariant set functions in high-dim scenarios. 

The key idea of the proof is to construct the embedding vector of the claimed length, and show the injectivity of any input set elements with the proposed embedding layers. The weights are used to (1) save the original features; (2) form an 'anchor' of the set element, which 'shares' the same permutation with the set element features; (3) store the coefficients that mix the original feature and the anchor.

Strengths:
- The manuscript is clearly written. The problem is sound and well-motivated.
- The results are significant, extending previous study to high-dimensions and exact representation of sets, in addition to tightening the upper bound from exponential to polynomial.

Weaknesses:
The major weakness of this study has been summarized in the limitation section already: (1) There are two important parameters in DeepSet, however only one is considered. (2) The lower bounds are trivial. (3) The upper bound depends on the specific neural network architecture.

Another issue is the proof is an existential argument. That is if we find those weights, the network can precisely represent the set function. However it is not clear if the weights belong to some space as a convergence by training the network. If the authors could provide intuition on this it would be good.

It took me longer than expected to understand the functionality of the three segments of the weights, especially the third one. I would suggest one more paragraph describing the mentality of the construction, together with the Eq 5,6,7.

From the technique side, there is no novel technique involved, and this together with the major weakness would be my personal reasonable potential to reject this paper. But fairly speaking I think the depth is OK, and the results are sufficiently impressive. Thus I recommend accept.

Questions:
- Line 223 and 340 indicate the lower bound of $L\geq ND$ for the LLE architecture, it may be better to add to line 63.
- In DeepSets, the $\rho$ and $\phi$ function can be implemented by several neural network layers of choice. Is function $\phi$ narrowed down to either 2 layers (LP) or 3 layers (LLE)? Or I believe at least the "linear" layer can be extended to multiple fully-connected layers. More generally, what is the flexibility in the network design during the implementation?
- This is out of curiosity only, I think it would be more intrigued to study the unconditional lower bound of this problem. Theorem 2.4 seems to address this when $D=1$. What is the barrier of obtaining an $\Omega(f(N,D))$ for general $D$?

Limitations:
Mentioned above.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "On Polynomial-Width Set Embeddings for High-Dimensional Data"  

**Overall Assessment:**  
This manuscript makes a valuable theoretical contribution by establishing that polynomial-width embedding layers are sufficient for exact representation of high-dimensional sets and permutation-equivariant functions. The work significantly extends prior results by reducing the upper bound from exponential to polynomial in \(N\) (set size) and \(D\) (dimension), leveraging novel injective architectures (LP/LLE). While the technical execution is largely solid and the core results are impactful, several limitations in scope and presentation warrant attention. I recommend **Weak Accept (6/10)**—this is a technically sound paper with moderate-to-high impact, though clarifications and minor expansions would strengthen its contribution.  

### Key Strengths:  
1. **Clarity and Motivation:** The problem is well-motivated, with clear connections to foundational work on set representation (e.g., DeepSets). The introduction effectively contextualizes the gap in high-dimensional guarantees.  
2. **Significant Theoretical Advancements:** The reduction of the upper bound to polynomial width (e.g., \(L \leq 2N^2D^2\) for LLE) is a major improvement over prior exponential bounds. The extension to exact reconstruction and permutation-equivariant functions via injective embeddings is compelling.  
3. **Architectural Innovations:** The LP (linear + power mapping) and LLE (logarithmic/linear/exponential) layers provide concrete, parameter-efficient designs for achieving injectivity. The anchor-based mixing strategy elegantly handles permutation invariance.  

### Key Weaknesses and Concerns:  
1. **Limited Scope of Analysis:** The upper bounds are restricted to specific LP/LLE architectures. A general bound for arbitrary architectures remains unexplored, limiting broader applicability.  
2. **Trivial Lower Bounds:** Lower bounds (e.g., \(L \geq ND\) for LLE) are straightforward and do not deepen our understanding of inherent complexity. Unconditional lower bounds for general \(D\) (analogous to Theorem 2.4 for \(D=1\)) are notably absent.  
3. **Existential Proofs and Training Dynamics:** The weight-construction proofs (e.g., Eqs. 5–7) are existential (i.e., weights *exist* but are not constructed algorithmically). Convergence guarantees during optimization are unaddressed, leaving practical feasibility unclear.  
4. **Technical Accessibility:** The weight-construction logic—especially the third group in Eq. 7—is challenging to follow. An intuitive explanation of the anchor-based mixing is needed to improve readability.  
5. **Mathematical Novelty:** While the proofs are rigorous (e.g., continuity via topological arguments on compact sets), they rely on established tools without introducing new techniques.  

### Suggestions for Improvement:  
1. **Clarify Lower Bounds:** Explicitly state in the main text (e.g., lines 223/340) that \(L \geq ND\) for LLE is a direct consequence of input dimensionality, not a profound limitation.  
2. **Discuss Architectural Flexibility:** Address whether LP/LLE layers can extend to multi-layer networks or other variants. This would clarify the trade-offs between width and depth.  
3. **Strengthen Lower Bounds:** Include unconditional lower bounds for general \(D\) to better contextualize the tightness of the polynomial upper bounds.  
4. **Improve Exposition:** Add a high-level overview of the weight-construction logic (Eqs. 5–7) in §3. Diagrams illustrating the "anchor-based mixing" concept could aid comprehension.  

### Main Contributions Recap:  
1. Polynomial width \(L\) suffices for exact set representation in \(D > 1\), a substantial improvement over exponential bounds.  
2. Novel LP/LLE layers ensure injectivity via anchor-based coordinate mixing.  
3. Extension to permutation-equivariant functions using existing theoretical machinery.  
4. Continuity of inverse maps under compactness assumptions enhances practical relevance.  

**Decision:**  
**Weak Accept (6/10)**. The paper’s tightening of set-representation bounds to polynomial width is a meaningful theoretical advance with implications for deep learning architectures. Though the analysis is confined to specific designs and some proofs lack clarity, the core results are technically sound and impactful. With minor revisions (especially in exposition), this work will be a valuable addition to the literature.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This manuscript proves that an embedding with polynomial width in the set size and feature dimension is sufficient to precisely reconstruct a set function, under some constraints on the embedding layer architecture. The main contribution is on the upper bound, which removes assumptions of previous studies, and reduces the gap exponentially. The scope of this study covers permutation-invariant and permutation-equivariant set functions in high-dim scenarios. 

The key idea of the proof is to construct the embedding vector of the claimed length, and show the injectivity of any input set elements with the proposed embedding layers. The weights are used to (1) save the original features; (2) form an 'anchor' of the set element, which 'shares' the same permutation with the set element features; (3) store the coefficients that mix the original feature and the anchor.

Strengths:
- The manuscript is clearly written. The problem is sound and well-motivated.
- The results are significant, extending previous study to high-dimensions and exact representation of sets, in addition to tightening the upper bound from exponential to polynomial.

Weaknesses:
The major weakness of this study has been summarized in the limitation section already: (1) There are two important parameters in DeepSet, however only one is considered. (2) The lower bounds are trivial. (3) The upper bound depends on the specific neural network architecture.

Another issue is the proof is an existential argument. That is if we find those weights, the network can precisely represent the set function. However it is not clear if the weights belong to some space as a convergence by training the network. If the authors could provide intuition on this it would be good.

It took me longer than expected to understand the functionality of the three segments of the weights, especially the third one. I would suggest one more paragraph describing the mentality of the construction, together with the Eq 5,6,7.

From the technique side, there is no novel technique involved, and this together with the major weakness would be my personal reasonable potential to reject this paper. But fairly speaking I think the depth is OK, and the results are sufficiently impressive. Thus I recommend accept.

Questions:
- Line 223 and 340 indicate the lower bound of $L\geq ND$ for the LLE architecture, it may be better to add to line 63.
- In DeepSets, the $\rho$ and $\phi$ function can be implemented by several neural network layers of choice. Is function $\phi$ narrowed down to either 2 layers (LP) or 3 layers (LLE)? Or I believe at least the "linear" layer can be extended to multiple fully-connected layers. More generally, what is the flexibility in the network design during the implementation?
- This is out of curiosity only, I think it would be more intrigued to study the unconditional lower bound of this problem. Theorem 2.4 seems to address this when $D=1$. What is the barrier of obtaining an $\Omega(f(N,D))$ for general $D$?

Limitations:
Mentioned above.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper studies the required neural network width for representing permutation invariant functions on sets. Existing works either focus on the case where the set elements are scalars, or require exponentially large neural network width with respect to the dimensions of the set elements. This work proves that, under certain assumptions, both the upper bound and lower bound on the required neural network are polynomial in the set size and set element dimensions.
reviews.strengths:
- The authors show that the bounds in this work are significantly better than existing results.

- This result implies that moderately wide networks are expressive enough to represent set functions.
reviews.weaknesses:
- **Lack of discussions on the practical implications of the results.** It seems that the main idea in the proof is to choose the mapping $\phi$ in a clever way. I'm curious about the practical implications of the constructions in the proof. Particularly, the authors mention _"neural networks to learn a set function have found a variety of applications in particle physics, computer vision and population statistics"_. I recommend the authors discuss how the results in this paper can provide insights and improvements on some typical network models in those areas (e.g., GNN, PointNet).

- **Lack of experimental verification.** Although the bounds in this work look significantly better than existing results, experimental verification would strengthen the argument.

- **Presentation issues.** I believe the authors should polish the presentation of Table 1 (comparisions with exsiting results), and the statement of the main theorem (Theorem 3.1)
   - Table 1:
      + $D+1$ and $D$ should be $N+1$ and $N$.
      + Use big-O notation to present the results of Segol et al. and Zweig & Brun.
      + I recommend to present the upper bound and lower bound separately as two columns.
      + The meaning of "Exact Rep." should be explained in the caption.
   - Theorem 3.1:
      + In lines 167 & 169, "For some" should be deleted as these two lines are part of the _where_ clause. 
      + Eq. (2) $w_1x\to w_1^{\top}x$. Otherwise please specify that $w_1, ..., w_K$ are row vectors.
      + The main result contains both upper bound and lower bound. I recommend stating these two separately. Particularly, the lower bound should be stated as a negative result. Theorem 2.4 in [1] is a good example of how the result should be rigorously formulated.
      + In the LLE architecture setting, one more assumption $\mathcal{K}\subseteq \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ is required. The authors should highlight this as a premise of the theorem, instead of putting it in the _where_ clause. 

      [1] Aaron Zweig and Joan Bruna. Exponential separations in symmetric neural networks.
- **Minor errors**.
   - Line 28: DeepSets$\ \ $[9] $\to$ DeepSets [9] (There are two spaces between "DeepSets" and "[9]" in the submission).
   - Line 117: suggests $\to$ suggest.

- Lines 189-191 state that the assumption $\mathcal{K}\subseteq \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ is not essential. Then why not remove the assumption and present a stronger result?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper studies the required neural network width for representing permutation invariant functions on sets. Existing works either focus on the case where the set elements are scalars, or require exponentially large neural network width with respect to the dimensions of the set elements. This work proves that, under certain assumptions, both the upper bound and lower bound on the required neural network are polynomial in the set size and set element dimensions.

Strengths:
- The authors show that the bounds in this work are significantly better than existing results.

- This result implies that moderately wide networks are expressive enough to represent set functions.

Weaknesses:
- **Lack of discussions on the practical implications of the results.** It seems that the main idea in the proof is to choose the mapping $\phi$ in a clever way. I'm curious about the practical implications of the constructions in the proof. Particularly, the authors mention _"neural networks to learn a set function have found a variety of applications in particle physics, computer vision and population statistics"_. I recommend the authors discuss how the results in this paper can provide insights and improvements on some typical network models in those areas (e.g., GNN, PointNet).

- **Lack of experimental verification.** Although the bounds in this work look significantly better than existing results, experimental verification would strengthen the argument.

- **Presentation issues.** I believe the authors should polish the presentation of Table 1 (comparisions with exsiting results), and the statement of the main theorem (Theorem 3.1)
   - Table 1:
      + $D+1$ and $D$ should be $N+1$ and $N$.
      + Use big-O notation to present the results of Segol et al. and Zweig & Brun.
      + I recommend to present the upper bound and lower bound separately as two columns.
      + The meaning of "Exact Rep." should be explained in the caption.
   - Theorem 3.1:
      + In lines 167 & 169, "For some" should be deleted as these two lines are part of the _where_ clause. 
      + Eq. (2) $w_1x\to w_1^{\top}x$. Otherwise please specify that $w_1, ..., w_K$ are row vectors.
      + The main result contains both upper bound and lower bound. I recommend stating these two separately. Particularly, the lower bound should be stated as a negative result. Theorem 2.4 in [1] is a good example of how the result should be rigorously formulated.
      + In the LLE architecture setting, one more assumption $\mathcal{K}\subseteq \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ is required. The authors should highlight this as a premise of the theorem, instead of putting it in the _where_ clause. 

      [1] Aaron Zweig and Joan Bruna. Exponential separations in symmetric neural networks.
- **Minor errors**.
   - Line 28: DeepSets$\ \ $[9] $\to$ DeepSets [9] (There are two spaces between "DeepSets" and "[9]" in the submission).
   - Line 117: suggests $\to$ suggest.

Questions:
- Can you discuss how the results in this paper can provide insights and improvements on some typical network models in those areas (e.g., GNN, PointNet)?

- Can you provide experimental verification of the result?

- Lines 189-191 state that the assumption $\mathcal{K}\subseteq \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ is not essential. Then why not remove the assumption and present a stronger result?

Limitations:
The limitations have been discussed in lines 375-377.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: Theoretical Bounds on the Width of Permutation-Invariant Neural Networks  

**Summary of the Paper and Review:**  
This paper establishes novel polynomial upper and lower bounds on the required width of neural networks for representing permutation-invariant set functions, significantly improving upon existing exponential bounds. While the theoretical contributions are substantial and technically sound, the work lacks discussion of practical implications, experimental validation, and contains notable presentation issues that affect clarity.  

### **Strengths:**  
1.  **Improved Theoretical Bounds:** The core contribution is significant. The paper proves substantially better upper and lower bounds ($N^5D^2$ and $N(D+1)$ for LP, $2N^2D^2$ and $ND$ for LLE) compared to prior exponential bounds, representing a meaningful theoretical advance.  
2.  **Implications for Expressivity:** The results demonstrate that moderately wide networks possess sufficient expressive power for representing permutation-invariant set functions, which is a valuable theoretical insight for the field.  

### **Weaknesses and Concerns:**  
1.  **Lack of Practical Context:** A significant omission is the discussion of how these improved bounds relate to practical applications and widely used models like GNNs or PointNet. The paper remains purely theoretical without grounding its findings in real-world implications or limitations for practitioners.  
2.  **Absence of Empirical Validation:** While the theoretical proofs are presented, there is no experimental validation (e.g., on synthetic or benchmark tasks) to empirically verify the derived bounds or explore their practical tightness. This limits the assessment of their real-world relevance.  
3.  **Presentation Issues in Key Results:**  
    *   **Table 1:** Contains several inaccuracies and lacks clarity. Specifically:  
        *   The entries for DeepSets/Wagstaff should use $N+1/N$ (input set size), not $D+1/D$ (dimensionality).  
        *   The results for Segol & Zweig and Bruna should be presented using big-O notation for consistency and accuracy.  
        *   The distinction between upper and lower bounds is unclear. Columns should be explicitly labeled as such.  
        *   The meaning of "Exact Rep." is ambiguous and requires clarification.  
    *   **Theorem 3.1:** Requires revision for precision:  
        *   The phrase "For some" should be removed from the statement.  
        *   The notation $w_1x$ is ambiguous and needs clarification (presumably $w_1^T x$).  
        *   The upper and lower bounds should be stated separately for improved readability.  
        *   The critical assumption $\mathcal{K}\subseteq\mathbb{R}_{>0}$ for the LLE architecture must be explicitly highlighted within the theorem statement.  
4.  **Minor Errors:** Several minor issues detract from polish, including spacing problems (e.g., line 28) and grammatical issues (e.g., "suggests" used incorrectly in line 117).  

### **Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Contextualize Practical Impact:** Add a dedicated discussion section (or significantly expand the introduction/conclusion) addressing the implications of these bounds for the design, analysis, or limitations of practical models like GNNs and PointNet. How do these polynomial bounds influence our understanding of their expressiveness or required scale?  
2.  **Incorporate Empirical Validation:** Supplement the theoretical contributions with experimental results. Simple experiments demonstrating the achievability (or challenging the tightness) of the bounds on controlled tasks would significantly strengthen the paper's impact and bridge theory and practice.  
3.  **Revise Key Presentation Elements:**  
    *   **Table 1:** Correct the DeepSets/Wagstaff entries to use $N$, use big-O notation consistently for Segol/Zweig and Bruna, clearly separate columns for upper and lower bounds, and define "Exact Rep."  
    *   **Theorem 3.1:** Implement the specific clarifications listed above (remove "For some", clarify notation, separate bounds, emphasize $\mathcal{K}\subseteq\mathbb{R}_{>0}$).  
4.  **Proofread Thoroughly:** Perform a careful proofreading pass to correct minor grammatical errors, formatting inconsistencies (like spacing), and ensure precise language throughout.  

### **Overall Assessment and Decision:**  
This paper makes a valuable theoretical contribution by establishing significantly improved polynomial bounds on the width required for permutation-invariance in neural networks, advancing beyond prior exponential bounds. This core technical strength is clear and merits publication. However, the work is currently limited by the lack of discussion regarding practical implications for widely-used architectures, the absence of any experimental validation, and significant presentational flaws in key results (Table 1 and Theorem 3.1). While these weaknesses are substantial, the importance of the theoretical advance provides sufficient grounds for acceptance provided the authors undertake major revisions, particularly addressing the presentation issues and adding meaningful discussion of practical relevance.  

**Decision: 5 (Borderline Accept)**  
*   **Reasons to Accept:** The derivation of polynomial bounds constitutes a significant and technically sound theoretical advance over existing exponential results.  
*   **Reasons to Reject (Mitigated by Revision Potential):** The lack of practical context, absence of experiments, and critical presentation flaws detract significantly from the paper's current impact and clarity. However, these are addressable through revision.  
*   **Recommendation:** **Accept** contingent on the authors satisfactorily addressing all presentation issues in Table 1 and Theorem 3.1, thoroughly proofreading the manuscript, and adding a substantive discussion on the practical implications (and limitations) of their bounds for models like GNNs and PointNet. While the addition of experimental validation is strongly encouraged, its absence is less critical for acceptance than addressing the presentation flaws and practical context. The revisions requested are essential for clarity and impact.

Summary:
This paper studies the required neural network width for representing permutation invariant functions on sets. Existing works either focus on the case where the set elements are scalars, or require exponentially large neural network width with respect to the dimensions of the set elements. This work proves that, under certain assumptions, both the upper bound and lower bound on the required neural network are polynomial in the set size and set element dimensions.

Strengths:
- The authors show that the bounds in this work are significantly better than existing results.

Weaknesses:
- **Lack of discussions on the practical implications of the results.** It seems that the main idea in the proof is to choose the mapping $\phi$ in a clever way. I'm curious about the practical implications of the constructions in the proof. Particularly, the authors mention _"neural networks to learn a set function have found a variety of applications in particle physics, computer vision and population statistics"_. I recommend the authors discuss how the results in this paper can provide insights and improvements on some typical network models in those areas (e.g., GNN, PointNet).

- **Lack of experimental verification.** Although the bounds in this work look significantly better than existing results, experimental verification would strengthen the argument.

- **Presentation issues.** I believe the authors should polish the presentation of Table 1 (comparisions with exsiting results), and the statement of the main theorem (Theorem 3.1)
   - Table 1:
      + $D+1$ and $D$ should be $N+1$ and $N$.
      + Use big-O notation to present the results of Segol et al. and Zweig & Brun.
      + I recommend to present the upper bound and lower bound separately as two columns.
      + The meaning of "Exact Rep." should be explained in the caption.
   - Theorem 3.1:
      + In lines 167 & 169, "For some" should be deleted as these two lines are part of the _where_ clause. 
      + Eq. (2) $w_1x\to w_1^{\top}x$. Otherwise please specify that $w_1, ..., w_K$ are row vectors.
      + The main result contains both upper bound and lower bound. I recommend stating these two separately. Particularly, the lower bound should be stated as a negative result. Theorem 2.4 in [1] is a good example of how the result should be rigorously formulated.
      + In the LLE architecture setting, one more assumption $\mathcal{K}\subseteq \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ is required. The authors should highlight this as a premise of the theorem, instead of putting it in the _where_ clause. 

      [1] Aaron Zweig and Joan Bruna. Exponential separations in symmetric neural networks.
- **Minor errors**.
   - Line 28: DeepSets$\ \ $[9] $\to$ DeepSets [9] (There are two spaces between "DeepSets" and "[9]" in the submission).
   - Line 117: suggests $\to$ suggest.

Questions:
- Can you discuss how the results in this paper can provide insights and improvements on some typical network models in those areas (e.g., GNN, PointNet)?

- Can you provide experimental verification of the result?

- Lines 189-191 state that the assumption $\mathcal{K}\subseteq \mathbb{R}_{>0}$ is not essential. Then why not remove the assumption and present a stronger result?

title: Exploring the Promise and Limits of Real-Time Recurrent Learning

reviews.summary:
The authors investigate Realtime Recurrent Learning (RTRL) applied to recurrent RL, in contrast with traditional Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). The authors' key insight is that they can significantly reduce time and space complexity by using single-layer elementwise recurrence. They propose an elementwise-LSTM (eLSTM), similar to IndRNN, for this purpose.

The authors execute a toy diagnostic task, then use IMPALA and R2D2 to train two variants of their eLSTM on pixels tasks -- one variant using BPTT and the other using RTRL.

As expected, the authors find that increasing the TBPTT length to large values provides the same performance as RTRL. Their is a notable performance gap when using short TBPTT, motivating the use of their approach. Although the tasks here do not necessarily require RTRL, one can imagine POMDPs that span hundreds of thousands of timesteps where this could be useful.
reviews.strengths:
- The authors present an interesting and forward-looking idea. Although we are not aware of any common RL tasks today that require such long-term backpropgatation, it's clear that human-level decision making happens over timeframes much too long for BPTT to be tractable and for TBPTT to capture such dependencies. We suspect methods like RTRL will be necessary for truly intelligent agents.
- The paper is generally well written, and it appears that the authors have dilligently reviewed prior literature.
reviews.weaknesses:
- My biggest complaint is that there is no data showing empirical time or space efficiency of RTRL. My understanding is that the whole point of RTRL is decoupling training space efficiency from the sequence length. I do not expect RTRL to be faster for such short sequences, but I would like to compare practical wall-clock time or GPU memory usage.

- I think it should be made explicit that in the traditional RL scenario (rollout workers separate from trainer, sync weights from trainer to workers every update), BPTT and RTRL are equivalent. It is really only the BPTT truncation that causes issues.

- The paper could benefit from a mini-study further examining the effects of TBPTT vs BPTT. The watermaze/chaser experiments do this, but only for a very limited number of truncation lengths. It would be interesting to see results with truncation lengths at 10, 20, ... 200.
reviews.questions:
- Line 24: to cache -> caching
- Diagnostic task is run for a maximum length of 1000
    - This is achievable using untruncated BPTT. Since BPTT scales with sequence length and RTRL does not, it would make more sense to do a very large sequence length.
- It is not clear to me why varying $M$ matters for RTRL if training is not "online" in the sense that the rollout workers are not updating weights
reviews.limitations: - RTRL is relatively understudied and thus has numerous limitations in its current state. As it receives further attention, I suspect such limitations will be mitigated. It is nice to see the authors be honest about the limitations of RTRL throughout the paper.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors investigate Realtime Recurrent Learning (RTRL) applied to recurrent RL, in contrast with traditional Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). The authors' key insight is that they can significantly reduce time and space complexity by using single-layer elementwise recurrence. They propose an elementwise-LSTM (eLSTM), similar to IndRNN, for this purpose.

The authors execute a toy diagnostic task, then use IMPALA and R2D2 to train two variants of their eLSTM on pixels tasks -- one variant using BPTT and the other using RTRL.

As expected, the authors find that increasing the TBPTT length to large values provides the same performance as RTRL. Their is a notable performance gap when using short TBPTT, motivating the use of their approach. Although the tasks here do not necessarily require RTRL, one can imagine POMDPs that span hundreds of thousands of timesteps where this could be useful.

Strengths:
- The authors present an interesting and forward-looking idea. Although we are not aware of any common RL tasks today that require such long-term backpropgatation, it's clear that human-level decision making happens over timeframes much too long for BPTT to be tractable and for TBPTT to capture such dependencies. We suspect methods like RTRL will be necessary for truly intelligent agents.
- The paper is generally well written, and it appears that the authors have dilligently reviewed prior literature.

Weaknesses:
- My biggest complaint is that there is no data showing empirical time or space efficiency of RTRL. My understanding is that the whole point of RTRL is decoupling training space efficiency from the sequence length. I do not expect RTRL to be faster for such short sequences, but I would like to compare practical wall-clock time or GPU memory usage.

- I think it should be made explicit that in the traditional RL scenario (rollout workers separate from trainer, sync weights from trainer to workers every update), BPTT and RTRL are equivalent. It is really only the BPTT truncation that causes issues.

- The paper could benefit from a mini-study further examining the effects of TBPTT vs BPTT. The watermaze/chaser experiments do this, but only for a very limited number of truncation lengths. It would be interesting to see results with truncation lengths at 10, 20, ... 200.

Questions:
- Line 24: to cache -> caching
- Diagnostic task is run for a maximum length of 1000
    - This is achievable using untruncated BPTT. Since BPTT scales with sequence length and RTRL does not, it would make more sense to do a very large sequence length.
- It is not clear to me why varying $M$ matters for RTRL if training is not "online" in the sense that the rollout workers are not updating weights

Limitations:
- RTRL is relatively understudied and thus has numerous limitations in its current state. As it receives further attention, I suspect such limitations will be mitigated. It is nice to see the authors be honest about the limitations of RTRL throughout the paper.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review: "Realtime Recurrent Learning for Recurrent Reinforcement Learning: Reducing the Complexity via Elementwise Recurrence"

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a timely investigation into Realtime Recurrent Learning (RTRL) for recurrent reinforcement learning, proposing an elementwise-LSTM (eLSTM) architecture to reduce RTRL's computational complexity. The core contribution lies in demonstrating RTRL's advantages over truncated Backpropagation Through Time (TBPTT) in specific memory tasks, particularly highlighting performance gaps with short truncation lengths. While the work is forward-looking and identifies RTRL as potentially crucial for future intelligent agents operating over very long timescales, the empirical evaluation currently shows limitations in demonstrating efficiency gains and selecting tasks that truly necessitate RTRL over sufficiently long TBPTT.

**Strengths:**  
1.  **Novelty and Significance:** The paper tackles a pertinent and forward-looking problem. RTRL remains understudied in RL, and its efficient implementation could be essential for developing agents capable of learning over extremely long sequences (e.g., hundreds of thousands of timesteps), making this work potentially impactful for future research on truly intelligent systems.
2.  **Clarity and Scholarship:** The paper is generally well-written, presenting concepts clearly. The literature review is diligent and situates the work appropriately within the existing landscape of recurrent learning methods in RL.
3.  **Transparency:** The authors provide an honest and thorough discussion of RTRL's inherent limitations throughout the paper, which is commendable and aids reader understanding.

**Weaknesses:**  
1.  **Lack of Efficiency Metrics:** A significant gap is the absence of empirical data demonstrating the practical time or space efficiency of the proposed eLSTM-RTRL approach compared to TBPTT baselines. Crucially missing are concrete measurements such as wall-clock training time per step/epoch and GPU memory usage under comparable conditions. This is vital for assessing RTRL's claimed practical benefits.
2.  **Equivalence Clarification:** The paper does not explicitly state the fundamental equivalence of BPTT and RTRL in the traditional RL setting (where separate rollout workers feed data to a central trainer, and weights are periodically synced), except for the critical difference introduced by truncation in TBPTT. This equivalence should be clearly articulated.
3.  **Limited TBPTT Analysis:** The examination of TBPTT's performance degradation with shorter truncation lengths is somewhat limited. Only a few specific truncation lengths are tested. A broader sweep (e.g., lengths of 10, 20, 50, 100, 200) would provide a more comprehensive understanding of the performance gap RTRL aims to bridge.
4.  **Task Suitability:** The chosen diagnostic and RL tasks (with maximum sequence lengths of 1000) do not convincingly *require* RTRL, as they can be adequately solved using TBPTT with a sufficiently long truncation length. This limits the demonstration of RTRL's unique necessity.

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1.  **Include Efficiency Benchmarks:** Provide empirical data comparing wall-clock time per step/epoch and GPU memory consumption between eLSTM-RTRL and TBPTT baselines (across various truncation lengths) on the same hardware. This is essential for validating the practical efficiency claims.
2.  **Clarify BPTT/RTRL Relationship:** Explicitly state in the main text that BPTT (without truncation) and RTRL are equivalent in the traditional RL setting (with separate rollout workers and a central trainer), differing primarily when TBPTT truncation is applied.
3.  **Expand TBPTT Length Study:** Conduct experiments evaluating TBPTT performance over a wider and finer-grained range of truncation lengths to better characterize the performance gap that motivates RTRL and to establish more precisely where RTRL becomes advantageous.
4.  **Utilize Longer Sequences:** To more compellingly showcase the unique advantage of RTRL, design and run experiments on tasks that involve sequence lengths significantly longer than 1000 timesteps – lengths that are impractical or impossible for standard BPTT to handle directly due to memory constraints.
5.  **Clarify Varying `M`:** In the context of non-online training (using separate rollout workers and a trainer), elaborate on the practical relevance and implementation considerations of varying the RTRL unroll length (`M`) during training. How does this parameter interact with the asynchronous data collection paradigm?
6.  **Minor Corrections:** Fix minor typos (e.g., line 24: change "to cache" to "caching").

**Overall Assessment and Recommendation:**  
This paper presents a technically solid contribution with a valuable core insight: reducing RTRL's complexity via elementwise recurrence (eLSTM) and demonstrating the potential of RTRL to outperform TBPTT when truncation is severe. While the current empirical evaluation has limitations regarding efficiency metrics, task selection, and the breadth of TBPTT comparison, the work is forward-looking, well-motivated, and addresses a significant challenge in recurrent RL. The honest discussion of limitations is commendable. The proposed approach and findings have moderate-to-high potential impact on the sub-areas of recurrent neural networks in RL and learning algorithms for long-term dependencies, particularly for future agents requiring online learning over vast timescales. The resources and methodology appear sufficient for reproducibility. Therefore, I recommend **Accept (Rating: 7)**. Addressing the suggested improvements, particularly adding efficiency benchmarks and extending the task analysis, would significantly strengthen the paper's contribution and impact.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors investigate Realtime Recurrent Learning (RTRL) applied to recurrent RL, in contrast with traditional Backpropagation Through Time (BPTT). The authors' key insight is that they can significantly reduce time and space complexity by using single-layer elementwise recurrence. They propose an elementwise-LSTM (eLSTM), similar to IndRNN, for this purpose.

The authors execute a toy diagnostic task, then use IMPALA and R2D2 to train two variants of their eLSTM on pixels tasks -- one variant using BPTT and the other using RTRL.

As expected, the authors find that increasing the TBPTT length to large values provides the same performance as RTRL. Their is a notable performance gap when using short TBPTT, motivating the use of their approach. Although the tasks here do not necessarily require RTRL, one can imagine POMDPs that span hundreds of thousands of timesteps where this could be useful.

Strengths:
- The authors present an interesting and forward-looking idea. Although we are not aware of any common RL tasks today that require such long-term backpropgatation, it's clear that human-level decision making happens over timeframes much too long for BPTT to be tractable and for TBPTT to capture such dependencies. We suspect methods like RTRL will be necessary for truly intelligent agents.
- The paper is generally well written, and it appears that the authors have dilligently reviewed prior literature.

Weaknesses:
- My biggest complaint is that there is no data showing empirical time or space efficiency of RTRL. My understanding is that the whole point of RTRL is decoupling training space efficiency from the sequence length. I do not expect RTRL to be faster for such short sequences, but I would like to compare practical wall-clock time or GPU memory usage.

- I think it should be made explicit that in the traditional RL scenario (rollout workers separate from trainer, sync weights from trainer to workers every update), BPTT and RTRL are equivalent. It is really only the BPTT truncation that causes issues.

- The paper could benefit from a mini-study further examining the effects of TBPTT vs BPTT. The watermaze/chaser experiments do this, but only for a very limited number of truncation lengths. It would be interesting to see results with truncation lengths at 10, 20, ... 200.

Questions:
- Line 24: to cache -> caching
- Diagnostic task is run for a maximum length of 1000
    - This is achievable using untruncated BPTT. Since BPTT scales with sequence length and RTRL does not, it would make more sense to do a very large sequence length.
- It is not clear to me why varying $M$ matters for RTRL if training is not "online" in the sense that the rollout workers are not updating weights

Limitations:
- RTRL is relatively understudied and thus has numerous limitations in its current state. As it receives further attention, I suspect such limitations will be mitigated. It is nice to see the authors be honest about the limitations of RTRL throughout the paper.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper explores an alternative to back-propagation through time (BPTT) for training recurrent neural networks, called real time recurrent learning (RTRL). The alternative algorithm does not depend on past activations, and also does not need truncation (which limits past context) like in BPTT to make it tractable. Although RTRL is very computationally complex for the general case, this paper exploits the fact that several recent recurrent architectures (Quasi-RNNs) allow for exact RTRL gradients to be computed efficiently. The method is evaluated on reinforcement learning tasks on several benchmarks and is compared with truncated BPTT  with LSTM while using Actor-Critic method as RL algorithms.
reviews.strengths:
1.	The paper studies a very interesting direction of using RTRL instead of truncated BPTT. The difficulty of using BPTT for training recurrent architectures is well known and it might be a potential bottleneck in allowing recurrent networks in achieving their full performance in complex tasks in the real-world.  
2.	The paper is written in a very clear way, clearly outlining the core contributions and the focus of this work.
3.	The proposed method is very clean, elegant, well-explained, and simple. 
4.	The empirical evaluation is done very extensively on DMLab, ProcGen and Atari, and it's interesting to see that RTRL + A2C performs equivalent, or better than TBPTT, except for in a few scenarios. 
5.	The limitations section is well-written and shows a good understanding of the proposed technique.
reviews.weaknesses:
Weaknesses / Questions:
1. Ablate the dependence of M (episode rollout length) on R2AC. Since larger M leads to less frequent updates and shorter M leads to stale values in the sensitivity matrix, it would be interesting to see how the performance varies with this parameter. 
2. One modification to the empirical evaluation that I would suggest is to compare with LSTM (instead of eLSTM), and also to compare the best performance for both the training algorithms – TBPTT and R2AC for any value of M, tuned separately for the two algorithms.
3. How does BPTT + LSTM with multiple hidden layers perform in the considered benchmarks?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper explores an alternative to back-propagation through time (BPTT) for training recurrent neural networks, called real time recurrent learning (RTRL). The alternative algorithm does not depend on past activations, and also does not need truncation (which limits past context) like in BPTT to make it tractable. Although RTRL is very computationally complex for the general case, this paper exploits the fact that several recent recurrent architectures (Quasi-RNNs) allow for exact RTRL gradients to be computed efficiently. The method is evaluated on reinforcement learning tasks on several benchmarks and is compared with truncated BPTT  with LSTM while using Actor-Critic method as RL algorithms.

Strengths:
1.	The paper studies a very interesting direction of using RTRL instead of truncated BPTT. The difficulty of using BPTT for training recurrent architectures is well known and it might be a potential bottleneck in allowing recurrent networks in achieving their full performance in complex tasks in the real-world.  
2.	The paper is written in a very clear way, clearly outlining the core contributions and the focus of this work.
3.	The proposed method is very clean, elegant, well-explained, and simple. 
4.	The empirical evaluation is done very extensively on DMLab, ProcGen and Atari, and it's interesting to see that RTRL + A2C performs equivalent, or better than TBPTT, except for in a few scenarios. 
5.	The limitations section is well-written and shows a good understanding of the proposed technique.

Weaknesses:
Weaknesses / Questions:
1. Ablate the dependence of M (episode rollout length) on R2AC. Since larger M leads to less frequent updates and shorter M leads to stale values in the sensitivity matrix, it would be interesting to see how the performance varies with this parameter. 
2. One modification to the empirical evaluation that I would suggest is to compare with LSTM (instead of eLSTM), and also to compare the best performance for both the training algorithms – TBPTT and R2AC for any value of M, tuned separately for the two algorithms.
3. How does BPTT + LSTM with multiple hidden layers perform in the considered benchmarks?

Questions:
(see weaknesses section for questions)

Limitations:
The limitations section is well-written and gives a clear explanation of the limits of the proposed line of work to train recurrent networks.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper presents a compelling exploration of Real-Time Recurrent Learning (RTRL) as an alternative to truncated backpropagation through time (BPTT) for training recurrent architectures. The authors demonstrate that RTRL, when applied to tractable element-wise recurrent networks (e.g., Quasi-RNNs), achieves competitive or superior performance on reinforcement learning benchmarks. The work addresses a significant gap in training recurrent models for long sequences while avoiding truncation constraints.  

**Strengths:**  
1. **Novelty and Impact:** The paper tackles a timely and impactful challenge by leveraging RTRL to bypass the truncation limitations of BPTT. This enables exact gradient computation for long sequences, opening avenues for more biologically plausible and efficient recurrent learning.  
2. **Clarity and Methodology:** The presentation is well-structured, with a clear exposition of the methodology. The implementation of RTRL using tractable architectures (e.g., element-wise LSTMs) is elegant and simplifies computational demands.  
3. **Rigorous Evaluation:** The empirical validation across diverse RL benchmarks (DMLab-30, ProcGen, Atari) is extensive and convincing. The results robustly demonstrate that RTRL-based methods (e.g., R2AC) match or exceed TBPTT baselines, particularly in memory-intensive tasks.  
4. **Transparency:** The thorough discussion of RTRL’s limitations—especially scalability in multi-layer settings and integration with non-recurrent components—adds valuable context and honesty to the work.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Lack of Ablation Studies:** The effect of the rollout length parameter (*M*) on performance is not analyzed. Given its role in controlling TD-learning steps and update frequency, ablations across *M* values would clarify its sensitivity.  
2. **Incomplete Baselines:** Comparisons are limited to eLSTM architectures. Omitting standard LSTMs trained via BPTT or RTRL makes it difficult to gauge the generalizability of the proposed approach.  
3. **Scalability Benchmarking:** The paper does not evaluate multi-layer BPTT with LSTMs, which would better contextualize the trade-offs between RTRL’s tractability and scalability in deeper architectures.  

**Suggestions for Improvement:**  
1. Conduct ablation studies on the hyperparameter *M* to quantify its impact on performance and training stability.  
2. Include comparisons with standard LSTMs (using both TBPTT and RTRL) to strengthen claims about architectural generality.  
3. Benchmark against multi-layer BPTT to assess how the proposed method scales with network depth.  

**Technical Quality:**  
The technical execution is sound. The use of element-wise recurrence (e.g., eLSTM) efficiently reduces RTRL’s computational complexity, and the integration of RTRL with actor-critic methods (R2AC) is well motivated for POMDPs. Experiments are well designed, leveraging diverse RL environments with varying sequence lengths to stress-test the approach.  

**Decision and Overall Assessment:**  
This is a technically solid and impactful contribution that advances training methods for recurrent networks in RL. The novel application of RTRL to tractable architectures addresses a critical limitation of BPTT, and the empirical validation is thorough. While the omission of certain baselines and ablations slightly limits the scope, the paper’s strengths in novelty, clarity, and empirical rigor outweigh these concerns. The work has high potential to influence both RL and recurrent modeling communities.  

**Rating: 7 (Accept)**  
*Justification:* The paper makes a high-impact contribution to training recurrent networks, offers excellent empirical evaluation, and transparently discusses limitations. It is reproducible, ethically sound, and merits acceptance despite minor weaknesses.

The alternative algorithm does not depend on past activations, and also does not need truncation (which limits past context) like in BPTT to make it tractable. reviews.summary:
- The paper explores the use of RTRL in the case of scenario, where no approximation is needed.
- The proposed "method" is not novel per se i.e., Mozer et. al put forward the derivation of RTRL for element-wise recurrence, but to the best of reviewer's ability no one has formally wrote about the tractability of the RTRL in case of some of the RNN architectures (Quasi-RNN or Simple Recurrent Units).
- The paper does experiments for RL, where on different experiments the paper shows the method trained with RTRL can match or even sometimes outperform Truncated Backprop through time. 

==== 

Read the author's rebuttal.
reviews.strengths:
- The paper is very well written.
- The idea of using RTRL for RNNs in which element-wise recurrent is present is interesting.
- The paper does experiments on many RL tasks, and compares the proposed method to TBPTT.
reviews.weaknesses:
- Since the paper is about how well RTRL works with some RNNs, it will be useful to evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling or sequential image classification etc. 
- For all the experiments, it will also be useful to report a BPTT baseline i.e., where we backprop through the entire "episode". 
- It will also be useful to compare to various other approximations of RTRL (using the vanilla LSTM/GRU) or even consider the tasks consider in SnAp-1. 
- Not a weakness per se, but this is incorrect "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity". DODGE work explores not just the random projections, but also learning the candidate direction (as a result of auxiliary tasks or via RL like synthetic gradients).
reviews.questions:
- Evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling, sequential image classification as well as compare to SnAp-1.
- Report the BPTT baseline, as well as some approximation of RTRL to just see how well those approximations work for the problems chosen in this work. 
- It will be helpful to make sure similar setup is followed throughout the experiments. For example: for some experiments, the paper uses the pre-trained visual encoder, or trains the visual encoder via the truncated BPTT gradient.
reviews.limitations: See Weaknesses and Questions.

Summary:
- The paper explores the use of RTRL in the case of scenario, where no approximation is needed.
- The proposed "method" is not novel per se i.e., Mozer et. al put forward the derivation of RTRL for element-wise recurrence, but to the best of reviewer's ability no one has formally wrote about the tractability of the RTRL in case of some of the RNN architectures (Quasi-RNN or Simple Recurrent Units).
- The paper does experiments for RL, where on different experiments the paper shows the method trained with RTRL can match or even sometimes outperform Truncated Backprop through time. 

==== 

Read the author's rebuttal.

Strengths:
- The paper is very well written.
- The idea of using RTRL for RNNs in which element-wise recurrent is present is interesting.
- The paper does experiments on many RL tasks, and compares the proposed method to TBPTT.

Weaknesses:
- Since the paper is about how well RTRL works with some RNNs, it will be useful to evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling or sequential image classification etc. 
- For all the experiments, it will also be useful to report a BPTT baseline i.e., where we backprop through the entire "episode". 
- It will also be useful to compare to various other approximations of RTRL (using the vanilla LSTM/GRU) or even consider the tasks consider in SnAp-1. 
- Not a weakness per se, but this is incorrect "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity". DODGE work explores not just the random projections, but also learning the candidate direction (as a result of auxiliary tasks or via RL like synthetic gradients).

Questions:
- Evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling, sequential image classification as well as compare to SnAp-1.
- Report the BPTT baseline, as well as some approximation of RTRL to just see how well those approximations work for the problems chosen in this work. 
- It will be helpful to make sure similar setup is followed throughout the experiments. For example: for some experiments, the paper uses the pre-trained visual encoder, or trains the visual encoder via the truncated BPTT gradient.

Limitations:
See Weaknesses and Questions.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Peer Review  

**Overall Assessment**  
This paper presents an interesting exploration of exact RTRL for RNNs with element-wise recurrence and demonstrates competitive performance with TBPTT in reinforcement learning tasks. While the core idea is technically sound and the writing is clear, the evaluation scope is insufficiently broad, and critical baselines are missing. These limitations substantially diminish the paper’s contribution, leading me to recommend **borderline rejection (Rating 4)**.  

### Strengths  
1. **Clarity and Presentation**: The paper is very well-written, with a logical structure and clear exposition of methodologies.  
2. **Conceptual Interest**: The focus on exact RTRL for RNNs with element-wise recurrence (e.g., UGRNN, SRU) is a compelling direction, avoiding approximations common in prior work.  
3. **Reinforcement Learning Experiments**: The RL experiments across multiple tasks (e.g., Minigrid, Memory Maze) provide valuable empirical validation, demonstrating competitive results against TBPTT.  

### Weaknesses and Major Concerns  
1. **Limited Evaluation Scope**:  
   - The experiments are confined to RL tasks. To robustly validate the method’s generality, evaluations on standard sequential modeling benchmarks (e.g., language modeling, sequential image classification) are essential.  
   - The absence of these benchmarks makes it difficult to assess the broader applicability of the proposed approach.  

2. **Inadequate Baselines**:  
   - **Missing BPTT Baseline**: No comparison to "full-episode" BPTT (backpropagating through entire episodes) is provided, which is a critical baseline for evaluating RTRL’s advantages in long sequences.  
   - **Lack of RTRL Approximation Comparisons**: The paper does not compare against existing RTRL approximations (e.g., SnAp-1, UORO, or vanilla LSTM/GRU with RTRL), limiting insights into trade-offs between exact and approximate methods.  

3. **Incomplete Related Work Context**:  
   - The core derivation of RTRL for element-wise recurrent architectures builds directly on prior work (e.g., Mozer et al.), yet this connection is not sufficiently acknowledged, overstating novelty.  
   - **Mischaracterization of Silver et al. [12] (DODGE)**: The paper inaccurately describes DODGE as solely relying on random projections, overlooking its use of auxiliary tasks/RL to *learn* candidate directions. This undermines the paper’s positioning against existing methods.  

4. **Technical Inconsistencies**:  
   - Experimental setups lack clarity, particularly regarding vision modules (pre-trained vs. TBPTT-trained), which may confound results.  

### Suggestions for Improvement  
1. **Expand Evaluation**: Include language modeling (e.g., Penn Treebank) and sequential image classification (e.g., Sequential MNIST/CIFAR) to demonstrate broader applicability.  
2. **Add Critical Baselines**:  
   - Report results for full-episode BPTT.  
   - Compare against RTRL approximations (SnAp-1, UORO) and vanilla LSTM/GRU with RTRL on the same tasks.  
3. **Clarify Technical Claims**:  
   - Explicitly contextualize the RTRL derivation relative to Mozer et al.  
   - Correct the description of DODGE [12] to acknowledge its use of learned directions via auxiliary tasks/RL.  
4. **Ensure Experimental Consistency**:  
   - Standardize training protocols (e.g., clarify whether vision modules are pre-trained or trained end-to-end with TBPTT/RTRL).  

### Recommendation  
While the paper addresses a meaningful challenge in RNN optimization, the **narrow evaluation and omission of key baselines severely limit its contribution**. The technical inaccuracies regarding prior work further weaken the narrative. For these reasons, I recommend **borderline rejection**. With expanded experiments, rigorous comparisons, and corrected contextualization, this work could become suitable for publication.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
- The paper explores the use of RTRL in the case of scenario, where no approximation is needed.
- The proposed "method" is not novel per se i.e., Mozer et. al put forward the derivation of RTRL for element-wise recurrence, but to the best of reviewer's ability no one has formally wrote about the tractability of the RTRL in case of some of the RNN architectures (Quasi-RNN or Simple Recurrent Units).
- The paper does experiments for RL, where on different experiments the paper shows the method trained with RTRL can match or even sometimes outperform Truncated Backprop through time. 

==== 

Read the author's rebuttal.

Strengths:
- The paper is very well written.
- The idea of using RTRL for RNNs in which element-wise recurrent is present is interesting.
- The paper does experiments on many RL tasks, and compares the proposed method to TBPTT.

Weaknesses:
- Since the paper is about how well RTRL works with some RNNs, it will be useful to evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling or sequential image classification etc. 
- For all the experiments, it will also be useful to report a BPTT baseline i.e., where we backprop through the entire "episode". 
- It will also be useful to compare to various other approximations of RTRL (using the vanilla LSTM/GRU) or even consider the tasks consider in SnAp-1. 
- Not a weakness per se, but this is incorrect "Silver et al. [12] explore random projections of the sensitivity". DODGE work explores not just the random projections, but also learning the candidate direction (as a result of auxiliary tasks or via RL like synthetic gradients).

Questions:
- Evaluate the proposed method on more tasks like language modelling, sequential image classification as well as compare to SnAp-1.
- Report the BPTT baseline, as well as some approximation of RTRL to just see how well those approximations work for the problems chosen in this work. 
- It will be helpful to make sure similar setup is followed throughout the experiments. For example: for some experiments, the paper uses the pre-trained visual encoder, or trains the visual encoder via the truncated BPTT gradient.

Limitations:
See Weaknesses and Questions.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.
reviews.strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.
reviews.weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723
reviews.questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185
reviews.limitations: The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.

Summary:
The paper modifies the LSTM architecture so that RTRL complexity, which is usually O(n^4), becomes O(n^2) where n is the number of recurrent units. This is achieved by constraining the recurrent weights to a diagonal structure. After performing some diagnostic tests, the paper presents experimental results using the proposed eLSTM in actor-critic RL settings, specifically DMLab, ProcGen, and Atari. The paper concludes with a discussion on the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case.

Strengths:
The paper is well written and clearly structured. 

While the ideas presented in the paper are not novel per se, the application of these ideas to a modern RNN architecture in the context of RL is quite novel. 

The work is quite relevant for the RNN/RL communities as unlocking RTRL for large-scale recurrent models is a long-standing problem and RL is an important field of application for RNNs. 

The paper discusses some rarely addressed limitations about the complexity of RTRL in the multi-layer case as well as some other interesting points.

Weaknesses:
The main weakness of the paper is the empirical evaluation of the method. This ranges from an incrompehensible selection of tasks over a severe lack of external baselines to insufficient evaluation methods (e.g., lack of significance testing). In the following, I will elaborate in greater detail on these and other shortcomings. 

On Procgen and Atari, the paper lacks comparison to state-of-the-art methods or any other external baselines. In general, the authors seem to confuse the concepts of ablation studies and external baselines. For instance, TBPTT on the eLSTM architecture is an ablation study while TBPTT on the fully recurrent LSTM architecture is a baseline (which is missing in all experiments but should be included; see, e.g., [3] for design choices). The authors should compare to state-of-the-art baselines in all environments, not just DMLab-30. 

The paper should experimentally explore the limitations arising from the diagonal constraint on the hidden-to-hidden interactions. This limitation competes with the bias in the gradient estimate used by the companion learning algorithm and SnAp-1 for LSTM. The authors should compare these RTRL variants in experiments to justify their proposed method as opposed to existing ones. 

The core mechanism described in Eq. (5) that reduces the RTRL complexity has been proposed in [1] and should be cited properly. 

In all RL experiments, the authors report mean and standard deviation over only 3 seeds (at least for DMLab-30 and Procgen; for Atari I didn't find any info) without any significance testing. In particular, the use of the standard deviation as a measure of uncertainty in combination with a low number of seeds has been critisized as bad practice and should be replaced by interval estimates such as IQM [2]. 

The paper states that it focuses on "RTRL-based algorithms beyond diagnostic tasks." However, it limits these experiments to the realm of RL, where the importance of memory is often unclear. The authors should investigate how the architectural changes affect performance in some standard supervised tasks. 

[1] Felix A. Gers, Jürgen Schmidhuber:
Recurrent Nets that Time and Count. IJCNN (3) 2000: 189-194

[2] Rishabh Agarwal, Max Schwarzer, Pablo Samuel Castro, Aaron C. Courville, Marc G. Bellemare:
Deep Reinforcement Learning at the Edge of the Statistical Precipice. NeurIPS 2021: 29304-29320

[3] Tianwei Ni, Benjamin Eysenbach, Ruslan Salakhutdinov:
Recurrent Model-Free RL Can Be a Strong Baseline for Many POMDPs. ICML 2022: 16691-16723

Questions:
The proposed eLSTM architecture does not use any cell state activation function. This does not affect the complexity of RTRL so I wonder why this slightly off-standard decision was made. 

Line 21, 53, and 290: shouldn't the word "quadratic" be replaced by "quartic"? 

Why did the authors dedicate resources to reactive tasks where no benefit are to be expected or an RNN might not be helpful to begin with? I had the impression that resources for experiments are limited. This exacerbates the question why reactive tasks were explored. 

The paper states its focus is "to evaluate learning with untruncated gradients rather than the potential for online learning." I wonder on what basis this decision was made as online updates could lead to high sample efficiency, one of the key problems in RL. I feel the paper missed a big chance by not researching this connection of online updates and sample efficiency. Instead, it focussed on reactive tasks. 

The paper states that "both the feedforward and LSTM baselines perform similarly" in Procgen Memory mode. However, [5] shows that the feedforward baseline is outperformed by methods using memory in 4/6 environments. Moreover, the Procgen paper [4] (see appendix H) shows that LSTM or framestacking outperforms feedforward in 4/6 envirnments even in non-memory mode. 

In summary, I really like the presented method (and the way it was presented) and believe that it bears quite some potential. However, the empirical evaluation is very weak and I do not believe it can be fixed in the short rebuttal period. Therefore, publication of the paper seems premature at this point. 

[4] Karl Cobbe, Christopher Hesse, Jacob Hilton, John Schulman:
Leveraging Procedural Generation to Benchmark Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2020: 2048-2056

[5] Fabian Paischer, Thomas Adler, Vihang P. Patil, Angela Bitto-Nemling, Markus Holzleitner, Sebastian Lehner, Hamid Eghbal-Zadeh, Sepp Hochreiter:
History Compression via Language Models in Reinforcement Learning. ICML 2022: 17156-17185

Limitations:
The authors give some interesting discussion on the limitation of their work, in particular the multi-layer RNN case.
